title: PAC Prediction Sets for Meta-Learning

reviews.summary:
This paper builds on PAC prediction sets by extending it to few-shot meta-learning settings, where there is assumed access to a i.i.d. sequence of tasks. In few-shot meta-learning, the goal is to adapt to a new task with only a few training examples. In order to hope to do this successfully, the learner is also exposed to a collection of similar tasks (i.e., i.i.d. from some distribution over tasks) before being presented with the test task. From a confidence point of view, providing theoretically valid, non-vacuous confidence sets for few-shot learning is difficult. The work of [1] first proposed a method of also using the task distribution to help derive meaningful conformal prediction sets, that have marginal coverage guarantees over the draw of calibration tasks and test task. This paper adopts this setting, but casts it under the framework of PAC prediction sets, which offer high probability guarantees about the expected mis-coverage over new tasks, conditional on observed calibration tasks. On a high-level, PAC sets is to CP sets as Meta-PAC sets are to Meta-CP sets, and offer a different set of (desireable) performance guarantees. The paper empirically validates the effectiveness (and theoretical properties) of their algorithm on simulated few-shot meta-learning datasets (i.e., K-shot N-way image classification and relation classification tasks), as well as a realistic heart attack prediction task (where the "tasks" are stratified by year and state).

[1] Few-shot conformal prediction with auxiliary tasks. https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.08898.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper is well-written (for the most part, see below) and the experiments are interesting. As the authors do well in pointing out, their PAC calibration framework offers several desireable guarantees that are not covered by the Meta-CP procedure. For example, Meta-PAC sets will be valid on average across all new tasks, rather than just on the (n + 1)th, marginalized over the draw of tasks {1, ..., n + 1}. Like standard PAC sets, this is nice, as it is often more practical to assume that the same fixed calibration set is reused for predictions across many future tasks. Therefore, even though prediction sets have been considered in a meta-learning setting before, this seems like a meaningful contribution.

Apart from a few specific comments/concerns, my main remark is on the readability of the presentation. Granted, I think the paper does an ok job considering the number of factors to consider simultaneously, but the notation is quite burdensome. It may be much easier to follow if a high-level, step-by-step description of what the authors want to accomplish and how they do it, is provided at the start of section 4---instead of jumping right into notation. For example, at a high-level, Meta-PAC sets are trying to find a single, shared threshold to use that 
1. On average over the random draw of calibration tasks that are used to choose this threshold...
2. and on average over the random choice of task that is used to evaluate this threshold...
3. and on average over predictions the model makes using this threshold on the new task...
4. The prediction set contains the correct label.

(Where we can replace "average" with high probability $1-\delta$, $1-\alpha$, $1-\epsilon$.)

I think this message is hinted at in various places in the manuscript, but it would be useful to say it plainly up front (obviously, not exactly in those words, but in simple, intuitive terms).

On to a few high-lighted strengths...

- The theoretical setting is appealing, and a reasonable and useful extension to Meta-CP (as stated above). 
- The calibration strategy seems correct and nice. At first I was concerned that computing PAC-thresholds for each of the calibration tasks was missing a factor of 1/N so that they are valid simultaneously, before applying the PAC method again on top of these values---as, with high probability, many $\hat{\tau}_{\epsilon, \alpha/2}(S_i, A_i)$ will _not_ be in $T_\epsilon(\theta_i, A_i)$ for large N. So we'd be calibrating the final threshold based on some "imposter" thresholds from a subset of tasks {1, ..., N}. However, my understanding is that since we only care about the output of a PAC method being i.i.d. (this is the r.v. O = PS-Binom(...) where O is valid w.p. 1 - alpha), not the output being valid itself, we do not actually need the PAC property to hold simulataneously over calibration tasks. Hence, the authors are able to avoid the 1/N factor in favor of two 1/2 factors (which is way more efficient). 
- Empirical results seem strong.

And a few weaknesses...

- It's kind of bothersome that a single threshold is used for _all_ downstream tasks; i.e., $\tau$ never gets updated, regardless of the size of the adaptation set A. In contrast, the Meta-CP method attempts to also estimate a data-dependent threshold (i.e., the adjusted quantile prediction). Therefore, when there is enough few-shot data, or significant variability across tasks, this could be better (in terms of being test-task conditionally). Here, the threshold never changes, regardless of whether the new task seems incredibly hard or incredibly easy.
- How tight are Clopper-Pearson based bounds when using small calibration task sizes? Assuming access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks seems rather unrealistic, apart from very specific scenarios. The theory is technically solid, but a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration). Thus I anticipate it to have only a relatively modest impact.
reviews.questions:
- In the experiments it seems that you calibrate using tasks sampled from N choose k base calbration classes, and test on N choose k base (disjoint) test classes. Samples from the second are not i.i.d. w.r.t the second?
 - Lines 171-174 I can't understand. From what I can see, you simply want to estimate a value t s.t. $P(\hat{\tau}_{\alpha/2, \delta}(S) \leq t) \geq 1 - \delta$. I'm not sure what $g$ is, and adding the dummy label also seems confusing.
reviews.limitations: The authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. It might be worth to expand on the last sentence of the conclusion: namely that people should understand that this algorithm is not a cure-all for few-shot confidence estimation, but one that relies on several strong assumptions, and only holds on average across new tasks. That said, it is better than nothing!
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper builds on PAC prediction sets by extending it to few-shot meta-learning settings, where there is assumed access to a i.i.d. sequence of tasks. In few-shot meta-learning, the goal is to adapt to a new task with only a few training examples. In order to hope to do this successfully, the learner is also exposed to a collection of similar tasks (i.e., i.i.d. from some distribution over tasks) before being presented with the test task. From a confidence point of view, providing theoretically valid, non-vacuous confidence sets for few-shot learning is difficult. The work of [1] first proposed a method of also using the task distribution to help derive meaningful conformal prediction sets, that have marginal coverage guarantees over the draw of calibration tasks and test task. This paper adopts this setting, but casts it under the framework of PAC prediction sets, which offer high probability guarantees about the expected mis-coverage over new tasks, conditional on observed calibration tasks. On a high-level, PAC sets is to CP sets as Meta-PAC sets are to Meta-CP sets, and offer a different set of (desireable) performance guarantees. The paper empirically validates the effectiveness (and theoretical properties) of their algorithm on simulated few-shot meta-learning datasets (i.e., K-shot N-way image classification and relation classification tasks), as well as a realistic heart attack prediction task (where the "tasks" are stratified by year and state).

[1] Few-shot conformal prediction with auxiliary tasks. https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.08898.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
The paper is well-written (for the most part, see below) and the experiments are interesting. As the authors do well in pointing out, their PAC calibration framework offers several desireable guarantees that are not covered by the Meta-CP procedure. For example, Meta-PAC sets will be valid on average across all new tasks, rather than just on the (n + 1)th, marginalized over the draw of tasks {1, ..., n + 1}. Like standard PAC sets, this is nice, as it is often more practical to assume that the same fixed calibration set is reused for predictions across many future tasks. Therefore, even though prediction sets have been considered in a meta-learning setting before, this seems like a meaningful contribution.

Apart from a few specific comments/concerns, my main remark is on the readability of the presentation. Granted, I think the paper does an ok job considering the number of factors to consider simultaneously, but the notation is quite burdensome. It may be much easier to follow if a high-level, step-by-step description of what the authors want to accomplish and how they do it, is provided at the start of section 4---instead of jumping right into notation. For example, at a high-level, Meta-PAC sets are trying to find a single, shared threshold to use that 
1. On average over the random draw of calibration tasks that are used to choose this threshold...
2. and on average over the random choice of task that is used to evaluate this threshold...
3. and on average over predictions the model makes using this threshold on the new task...
4. The prediction set contains the correct label.

(Where we can replace "average" with high probability $1-\delta$, $1-\alpha$, $1-\epsilon$.)

I think this message is hinted at in various places in the manuscript, but it would be useful to say it plainly up front (obviously, not exactly in those words, but in simple, intuitive terms).

On to a few high-lighted strengths...

- The theoretical setting is appealing, and a reasonable and useful extension to Meta-CP (as stated above). 
- The calibration strategy seems correct and nice. At first I was concerned that computing PAC-thresholds for each of the calibration tasks was missing a factor of 1/N so that they are valid simultaneously, before applying the PAC method again on top of these values---as, with high probability, many $\hat{\tau}_{\epsilon, \alpha/2}(S_i, A_i)$ will _not_ be in $T_\epsilon(\theta_i, A_i)$ for large N. So we'd be calibrating the final threshold based on some "imposter" thresholds from a subset of tasks {1, ..., N}. However, my understanding is that since we only care about the output of a PAC method being i.i.d. (this is the r.v. O = PS-Binom(...) where O is valid w.p. 1 - alpha), not the output being valid itself, we do not actually need the PAC property to hold simulataneously over calibration tasks. Hence, the authors are able to avoid the 1/N factor in favor of two 1/2 factors (which is way more efficient). 
- Empirical results seem strong.

And a few weaknesses...

- It's kind of bothersome that a single threshold is used for _all_ downstream tasks; i.e., $\tau$ never gets updated, regardless of the size of the adaptation set A. In contrast, the Meta-CP method attempts to also estimate a data-dependent threshold (i.e., the adjusted quantile prediction). Therefore, when there is enough few-shot data, or significant variability across tasks, this could be better (in terms of being test-task conditionally). Here, the threshold never changes, regardless of whether the new task seems incredibly hard or incredibly easy.
- How tight are Clopper-Pearson based bounds when using small calibration task sizes? Assuming access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks seems rather unrealistic, apart from very specific scenarios. The theory is technically solid, but a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration). Thus I anticipate it to have only a relatively modest impact.

Questions:
- In the experiments it seems that you calibrate using tasks sampled from N choose k base calbration classes, and test on N choose k base (disjoint) test classes. Samples from the second are not i.i.d. w.r.t the second?
 - Lines 171-174 I can't understand. From what I can see, you simply want to estimate a value t s.t. $P(\hat{\tau}_{\alpha/2, \delta}(S) \leq t) \geq 1 - \delta$. I'm not sure what $g$ is, and adding the dummy label also seems confusing.

Limitations:
The authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. It might be worth to expand on the last sentence of the conclusion: namely that people should understand that this algorithm is not a cure-all for few-shot confidence estimation, but one that relies on several strong assumptions, and only holds on average across new tasks. That said, it is better than nothing!

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper tackles the problem of constructing confidence sets in few-shot meta-learning by extending PAC prediction sets to the meta-learning regime. Building on prior work in conformal prediction for meta-learning [1], the authors propose Meta-PS, a two-stage “meta-PAC” calibration procedure. First, for each of N calibration tasks, a meta-learned score function is adapted to the task and a PAC threshold is estimated via any off-the-shelf PAC prediction-set method. Second, these N thresholds are aggregated by a Clopper–Pearson–based interval to yield a single global threshold. The authors prove that this algorithm satisfies an (ε,α,δ)-meta-PAC guarantee, controlling the mis-coverage probability over calibration tasks, per-task adaptation sets, and test examples on new tasks. Empirically, Meta-PS is evaluated on K-shot N-way image classification (mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C), relation classification (FewRel), and a heart-attack prediction dataset (CDC heart). Results demonstrate that Meta-PS attains the target coverage level while producing smaller average set sizes than baselines such as pooled PAC, test-set PAC, and meta-conformal prediction.

Strengths:
- Novelty and Significance: The paper introduces a precise meta-PAC framework that extends classical PAC prediction sets to meta-learning. This three-parameter guarantee—over calibration tasks, adaptation sets, and test examples—is, to our knowledge, new and addresses a critical need for reliable uncertainty estimates in safety-critical few-shot settings.
- Theoretical Rigor: The derivation of the (ε,α,δ)-meta-PAC guarantee is clear and mathematically sound. By avoiding the 1/N slack factor (common in standard simultaneous PAC calibration) in favor of two 1/2 factors, the method is more sample-efficient than naïvely stacking PAC procedures.
- Empirical Breadth: Experiments span vision (mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C), language (FewRel), and healthcare (CDC heart dataset), showing consistent target coverage and improved set-size efficiency. Comparisons include appropriate baselines: pooled PAC, test-set PAC, and meta-conformal methods.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:
- Presentation and Notation: Section 4 plunges immediately into heavy notation, which may impede accessibility. A concise, step-by-step overview of Meta-PS—outlining the two calibration stages, conditioning events, and target guarantees in plain language—would greatly improve readability. Including pseudocode in the main text and a figure that illustrates the flow of random variables (calibration tasks → thresholds → aggregation) would further aid comprehension.
- Single Global Threshold: Meta-PS uses a fixed threshold for all future tasks, regardless of adaptation-set size or apparent task difficulty. While practical for reusing a calibration set, this may be suboptimal when tasks vary widely in complexity. Exploring an adaptive, data-dependent threshold (as in meta-conformal methods) or augmenting the scalar threshold parameterization could yield tighter sets in favorable scenarios.
- Sample Complexity and Calibration Budget: All reviews concur that the required number of calibration tasks (N) and per-task samples (n) may be large for realistic applications. The manuscript lacks quantitative guidance on how N and n must scale with (ε,α,δ) to achieve non-vacuous bounds. Including either theoretical sample-complexity rates or empirical sensitivity analyses (e.g., coverage and set-size as functions of N, n) would strengthen practical adoption.
- i.i.d. Task Assumption and Robustness: The meta-PAC guarantee rests on the assumption that calibration and test tasks are exchangeable draws from the same distribution. Real-world scenarios often involve domain shift or non-exchangeable tasks. An empirical study of Meta-PS under mild distributional shifts, or a discussion on diagnostic checks when this assumption fails, would clarify its limits.
- Computational Overhead and Reproducibility: The two-stage calibration entails repeated calls to PS-Binom or similar PAC subroutines, raising questions about runtime and memory consumption for large N. Profiling the computational cost relative to single-stage methods and releasing code and scripts would facilitate reproducibility and gauge scalability.

Detailed Comments:
1. Theoretical Clarification (Lines 171–174): The role of the auxiliary function g and the dummy label in the definition of the second-stage PAC procedure remains opaque. A brief remark linking g to the goal of bounding P(τ̂ ≤ t) ≥ 1 – δ would help.  
2. Experimental Protocols: It appears that calibration tasks sample classes from an N-choose-K pool, distinct from test classes. Clarify whether the sampled tasks remain i.i.d. under this protocol and discuss potential dependencies.  
3. Negative Societal Impact: While the conclusion notes that Meta-PS is not a panacea, a more substantive discussion of risks—such as over-reliance on average coverage guarantees in clinical settings or misinterpretation of high-probability statements—would be valuable.

Questions for the Authors:
1. Can you provide empirical curves showing how coverage and average set size vary with calibration budget (N, n)?  
2. Have you observed instability in the second-stage interval estimation when the per-task thresholds exhibit high variance?  
3. How sensitive is Meta-PS to moderate violations of the i.i.d. task assumption?  
4. Could the aggregation step be extended to multi-dimensional threshold parameters or non-scalar set constructions?  
5. What are the wall-clock runtime and memory overheads of Meta-PS compared to pooled PAC and meta-conformal baselines?

Conclusion and Rating:
Overall, this paper presents a technically solid and moderately impactful extension of PAC prediction sets to the meta-learning setting. While certain aspects—most notably presentation clarity, calibration budget requirements, and robustness under task heterogeneity—warrant further elaboration, the core theoretical contribution and empirical validation are sound. I recommend a Weak Accept (rating: 6), as the work is of interest to the uncertainty-quantification and meta-learning communities and lays a foundation for future refinements.

Strengths:


* The paper is well-written.
* The proposed method is reasonable with theoretical analysis.
* Experiments on various domain and datasets are compared.


Weakness:



* For reusing the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation, would be better if the meta predictions set parameters are also adapted?

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a PAC prediction set algorithm for meta learning. The goal is to adapt the predictor to new unseen tasks with a few labeled training examples. Experiments on four datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods with  PAC guarantee while having smaller prediction set size.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:


* The paper is well-written.
* The proposed method is reasonable with theoretical analysis.
* Experiments on various domain and datasets are compared.


Weakness:



* For reusing the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation, would be better if the meta predictions set parameters are also adapted?
* The current methods seem only applies on metric-based meta-learning methods, e.g., prototypical network. It would be better if the methods could be also applies on optimization-based methods, such as model-agnostic meta learning (MAML).
* For mini-ImageNet datasets, only 5-way classification problem is compared. It would be better if the authors can compare on more challenging, 10-way or 20-way classification.

Questions:
Please refer to weaknesss above.

Limitations:
Yes, the authors have addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.

6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper presents Meta-PS, a novel algorithm that extends PAC prediction sets to the meta‐learning setting. By meta‐training a score function across a collection of tasks and then performing a two‐stage “meta‐calibration”—first estimating per‐task PAC thresholds on calibration tasks, then constructing an overarching PAC interval over those thresholds—the method guarantees that, with high probability, prediction sets on new tasks achieve the target miscoverage rate while remaining small. The authors provide a clear definition of the (ε,α,δ)-meta-PAC guarantee, offer a concise proof sketch, and empirically validate Meta-PS on four benchmarks (mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C, FewRel, and a CDC heart-disease dataset), demonstrating reliable coverage and reduced set sizes compared to pooled PAC, test‐set PAC, and meta-conformal baselines.

Strengths:
1. Clarity and Writing: The paper is well-written and logically structured. Technical sections systematically develop notation, and key ideas—such as the two‐stage calibration flow—are illustrated with informative figures.
2. Theoretical Rigor: The (ε,α,δ)-meta-PAC framework is precisely defined, and correctness is supported by a rigorous proof. This extends classical PAC and conformal prediction theory in a meaningful way.
3. Practical Simplicity: Meta-PS builds directly on existing PAC set procedures. Once a meta‐learned score function is available, implementing the two calibration stages requires only minor adaptations.
4. Broad Empirical Evaluation: Experiments span vision, language, and medical domains, with comparisons to strong baselines. Results consistently show that Meta-PS meets coverage guarantees and yields smaller average set sizes.
5. Impact: Providing per-task coverage guarantees post-adaptation addresses a crucial need for uncertainty quantification in safety-critical few-shot learning scenarios.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:
1. Adaptation of Calibration Parameters:
   – As noted in the main review, Meta-PS reuses a single scalar threshold learned during meta‐calibration for all new tasks. Alternative Review 2 and 4 suggest that adapting this threshold post-adaptation (rather than reusing it unchanged) might further tighten set sizes. The authors could explore a hybrid strategy where the meta-interval informs a prior over task‐specific thresholds that are then fine-tuned.
2. Dependence on Metric‐Based Meta‐Learning:
   – Currently, all experiments use prototypical networks. Alternative Review 2 and 4 recommend evaluating Meta-PS with optimization‐based backbones (e.g., MAML) to ensure generality across meta‐learning paradigms. A small ablation on MAML would strengthen claims of broad applicability.
3. Calibration Budget and Sample Complexity:
   – Multiple alternative reviews flag the lack of guidance on choosing the number of calibration tasks (N) and per‐task sample size (n). A sensitivity analysis showing how coverage and set size vary with N and n would help practitioners understand resource trade‐offs. Theoretical bounds or empirical curves would be particularly valuable.
4. Task Heterogeneity and i.i.d. Assumption:
   – The (ε,α,δ)-meta-PAC guarantee relies on tasks being exchangeable draws from a common distribution. Yet in real applications, calibration and test tasks may exhibit covariate shift. Alternative Review 1 and 3 urge evaluation under distributional mismatch to assess robustness, or at least a discussion of how guarantees degrade when the i.i.d. assumption is violated.
5. Scalability and Computational Overhead:
   – While Meta-PS is conceptually simple, the two‐stage calibration (repeated PAC threshold estimation and interval construction) could incur nontrivial runtime costs for large N. Alternative Review 1 and 3 recommend profiling wall‐clock time, memory usage, and overall overhead compared to single‐stage PAC methods.
6. Extension Beyond Scalar Thresholds:
   – The paper focuses on scalar thresholds for set construction. As Alternative Review 4 notes, it may be fruitful to consider multidimensional or structured set parameters. At minimum, a discussion of this limitation and how the meta‐interval step might generalize would be helpful.
7. Multi‐way Classification Beyond 5‐way:
   – On mini-ImageNet, only 5-way tasks are evaluated. Following the main review’s point and Alternative Review 1 and 2, experiments on more challenging 10-way or 20-way episodes would better demonstrate scalability in realistic few-shot settings.
8. Societal Impact and Misinterpretation:
   – The authors acknowledge potential negative impacts, but as several reviewers point out, over-reliance on high‐probability guarantees without validating distributional assumptions can be risky—especially in clinical contexts. More concrete guidelines or diagnostic checks for practitioners would mitigate misuse.

Questions for the Authors:
1. How should practitioners choose N and n to achieve desired (ε,α,δ) levels under tight resource constraints? Can you provide empirical or theoretical guidance?
2. Have you evaluated Meta-PS on tasks that violate the i.i.d. assumption, such as domain shifts between calibration and test tasks? How do guarantees degrade?
3. What is the runtime and memory overhead of the two‐stage calibration compared to baseline PAC or conformal methods?
4. Would adapting the prediction-set threshold after test‐time adaptation (rather than reusing the meta-threshold directly) yield tighter sets? Have you tried a small ablation?
5. Do your results extend to optimization‐based meta‐learning methods (e.g., MAML) or higher-way classification episodes?

Decision and Rating:
6: Weak Accept. Meta-PS is technically solid, presents a well-motivated extension of PAC prediction sets to meta-learning, and offers valuable theoretical guarantees. Although the paper would benefit from deeper analysis of calibration budgets, task heterogeneity, and computational scalability—and broader validation across meta-learning paradigms—the core contributions are meaningful and the work merits publication with minor revisions.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a PAC prediction set algorithm for meta learning. The goal is to adapt the predictor to new unseen tasks with a few labeled training examples. Experiments on four datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods with  PAC guarantee while having smaller prediction set size.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:


* The paper is well-written.
* The proposed method is reasonable with theoretical analysis.
* Experiments on various domain and datasets are compared.


Weakness:



* For reusing the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation, would be better if the meta predictions set parameters are also adapted?
* The current methods seem only applies on metric-based meta-learning methods, e.g., prototypical network. It would be better if the methods could be also applies on optimization-based methods, such as model-agnostic meta learning (MAML).
* For mini-ImageNet datasets, only 5-way classification problem is compared. It would be better if the authors can compare on more challenging, 10-way or 20-way classification.

Questions:
Please refer to weaknesss above.

Limitations:
Yes, the authors have addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper considers the notions and algorithms for generating a prediction set under the meta learning setting that satisfies PAC guarantees. Since there are multiple distributions involved in meta learning setting, the considered PAC notion for meta learning is different from existing notion designed for conventional machine learning setting, such as [3]. Furthermore, the meta learning setting is also different from covariate shift case considered in [6]. For example, adaptation data sets are available for a trained model to adapt these associated distributions without estimation of importance weights. Therefore, meta-PAC (mPAC) notion is proposed (Definition 3), which uses an augmented calibration set from N distributions, i.e., $p_{\theta_1}, …, p_{\theta_N}$ to generate $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ whp $1-\delta$. Algorithm 1 is proposed to determine the parameter $\tau$ of prediction set, which uses a two-step strategy to first determine ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimator/threshold on each of N distributions, and then use these thresholds (with a label 1 attached to each threshold) to construct a new sample of size N to determine a threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC. Theorem 1 shows the guarantee of this algorithm for ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Originality:
This paper generalizes PAC notation of conformal prediction from previous studies to mPAC in meta learning case, where multiple distributions are present, augmented calibration set is available. Algorithm is proposed to guarantee mPAC built on the existing algorithm designed for PAC prediction. 

Quality:
This paper is technically sound and theoretical analysis is clear.

Clarity:
This paper is easy to follow.

Significance: 
This paper provides insights to conformal prediction in meta learning setting.

Questions:
Algorithm 2 involves grid search for $\tau$. Could the authors elaborate a bit more on its computational cost/efficiency in analysis or experiment (e.g., the Granularity for searching)? Would there be any possible way to accelerate such grid search?

Limitations:
I think the authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper addresses the important problem of constructing PAC‐guaranteed prediction sets in the meta‐learning setting, where one seeks reliable uncertainty quantification after fast adaptation to a new task. Building on classical PAC conformal prediction, the authors introduce the mPAC notion (Definition 3) to capture a three‐parameter guarantee—over calibration tasks, adaptation sets, and test examples—and propose Meta-PS (Algorithm 1) to realize an (ε,α,δ)‐mPAC prediction set. Meta-PS performs a two‐stage calibration: first, it obtains per‐task thresholds via any standard PAC predictor on N calibration tasks; second, it aggregates these thresholds into a meta‐level threshold using a PAC interval over the N values. Theorem 1 rigorously establishes that this procedure controls miscoverage at level ε with confidence 1–δ and error budget α.

Strengths:
1. Originality and Significance:
   - The paper generalizes single‐task PAC prediction sets to the meta‐learning regime, formulating a new (ε,α,δ)‐meta‐PAC guarantee that simultaneously accounts for randomness in calibration tasks, adaptation, and test data. This precise meta‐PAC notion and its two‐stage calibration algorithm fill a clear gap in uncertainty quantification for few‐shot adaptation.
   - By relying only on a score function (potentially learned via any meta‐learning backbone), Meta-PS is broadly applicable across vision, language, and medical tasks. Empirical results on mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C, FewRel, and a CDC heart‐disease dataset demonstrate that Meta-PS meets the desired coverage while often producing tighter prediction sets than baselines (pooled PAC, test‐set PAC, and meta‐conformal methods).

2. Theoretical Soundness:
   - The mPAC definition (Section 3) and the correctness proof for Algorithm 1 (Theorem 1) are technically rigorous and clearly presented. The two‐stage PAC construction leverages classical binomial‐based thresholding in a novel meta‐hierarchical fashion.
   - The proof sketch in the appendix and the step‐by‐step conditioning arguments convincingly show that the three sources of randomness are handled without overlap, yielding valid coverage guarantees.

3. Clarity and Presentation:
   - The paper is generally easy to follow, with a logical flow from motivation to formal definition, algorithm, theorem, and experiments. Figures illustrating the conditioning relationships between calibration tasks, adaptation sets, and test data aid comprehension.
   - Related work is well cited, with thorough discussion of standard PAC prediction, conformal and meta-conformal methods, and how mPAC differs from prior covariate‐shift approaches.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:
1. Sample Complexity and Practical Calibration Budgets:
   - While reviewers appreciated the generality of the meta-PAC framework, multiple comments noted the absence of explicit guidance or bounds on how the number of calibration tasks N and per‐task sample size n must scale to achieve practical (ε,α,δ) levels. A concrete discussion—either theoretical or through sensitivity plots—would help practitioners choose N and n under resource constraints.

2. Computational Efficiency:
   - Algorithm 2’s grid search for the meta‐threshold τ is conceptually simple but may incur nontrivial overhead for large N or fine granularity. A profiling study reporting wall‐clock time and memory usage relative to single‐stage PAC methods would clarify the trade‐off between tighter sets and computational cost.
   - Incorporating pseudocode for both stages into the main text, along with remarks on typical search ranges and step sizes, would strengthen reproducibility and guide implementation.

3. Robustness to Task Heterogeneity:
   - The i.i.d. task assumption (exchangeability of calibration and test tasks) underpins the mPAC guarantee. In real‐world meta‐learning, tasks may exhibit domain shift or non-exchangeability. Empirical robustness studies—e.g., deliberately varying the meta-distribution between calibration and test tasks—could illuminate how guarantees degrade in practice and suggest mitigation strategies.

4. Notation and Presentation Details:
   - Some technical notation (e.g., the construction of the augmented calibration set and score‐function g) is dense. Streamlining or more thoroughly annotating symbols in Section 4, and ensuring all figures reference defined terms, would improve readability.

5. Broader Impact and Reproducibility:
   - The authors briefly acknowledge potential societal risks of misinterpreting high‐probability coverage guarantees. A more detailed cautionary note or diagnostic checklist (e.g., on detecting meta‐distribution shift) would help mitigate over‐reliance in safety‐critical settings.
   - To facilitate adoption, a clear timeline for code release, along with comprehensive scripts and hyperparameter settings, is highly recommended.

Questions for the Authors:
1. Can you provide theoretical or empirical guidance on how N and n should scale with ε,α,δ to achieve reasonable coverage in practice?
2. What is the observed runtime and memory overhead of the two‐stage calibration compared to a single PAC threshold estimation?
3. Have you tested Meta-PS under synthetic or real task‐shift scenarios to assess sensitivity to violations of the i.i.d. task assumption?
4. Could the threshold‐aggregation step be extended beyond scalar τ to richer parameterizations (e.g., vector thresholds for multivariate score functions)?
5. How stable is the second‐stage PAC interval when per-task thresholds vary widely—did you observe any instability or outliers in τ estimation?

Limitations:
The requirement for a substantial number of calibration tasks and adaptation samples may limit applicability in truly low-data regimes. Moreover, the current framework assumes exchangeable tasks and scalar thresholding, which may not cover all meta-learning applications (e.g., regression or structured outputs). Future work could explore sample-efficient calibration, robustness to task heterogeneity, and multidimensional set constructions.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept. This paper delivers a technically sound and significant extension of PAC prediction sets to meta-learning. While the core contributions are solid, further analysis of sample complexity, computational costs, and robustness would strengthen practical impact.

reviews.summary:
POST DISCUSSION:

The authors addressed most of my issues about readability and the attribution problem, so I have increased my score to a weak accept.

My final assessment is as follows:
I believe the proofs are correct, and the related work is correct.
I think the paper is the first to do a two-stage training-conditional guarantee, which has some value. Although this guarantee is a straightforward extension of existing results, perhaps somebody might be interested in it, and I do not believe it has appeared before.

I also believe it contributes by being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal for meta-learning. It is slightly different from the existing work by Adam Fisch et al. in that it also conditions on the tasks. Maybe somebody will find that useful!

_____________
This paper introduces a form of distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee into meta-learning and proposes an algorithm to achieve it. The guarantee is a triply-nested one, where the innermost integral is an expectation on a 0-1 loss. This expectation is nested within two other probabilities, conditioned on the calibration set and the meta learned parameters.  The algorithm boils down to two applications of binomial concentration. There are then extensive experiments on several meta learning tasks in vision, language, and medicine.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths: I the technical results are clearly correct. The experiments are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used.  It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel.  The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.
reviews.questions: What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?
reviews.limitations: I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

I also believe it contributes by being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal for meta-learning. It is slightly different from the existing work by Adam Fisch et al. in that it also conditions on the tasks. Maybe somebody will find that useful!

This paper introduces a form of distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee into meta-learning and proposes an algorithm to achieve it. The guarantee is a triply-nested one, where the innermost integral is an expectation on a 0-1 loss. This expectation is nested within two other probabilities, conditioned on the calibration set and the meta learned parameters.  The algorithm boils down to two applications of binomial concentration. There are then extensive experiments on several meta learning tasks in vision, language, and medicine.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths: I the technical results are clearly correct. The experiments are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used.  It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.

Questions:
What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?

Limitations:
I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)

reviews.text:
Summary:  
This paper presents Meta-PS, a two-stage meta-calibration procedure that brings distribution-free uncertainty quantification to meta-learning. Building on training-conditional PAC prediction sets, the authors first adapt a meta-learned score function on each of N calibration tasks to select per-task thresholds via a PAC set method, and then apply a second binomial concentration argument to those thresholds to obtain a meta-level interval. They prove an (ε,α,δ)-meta-PAC guarantee controlling error over calibration tasks, adaptation sets, and test examples. Empirically, Meta-PS is evaluated on mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C, FewRel, and a CDC heart-disease dataset, consistently achieving target coverage while often producing smaller sets than pooled PAC, test-set PAC, and meta-conformal baselines. After the authors’ revisions on readability and attribution, I find the technical results correct and the work worthy of a weak accept.

Strengths:  
• Novel extension: As the first two-stage training-conditional guarantee in meta-learning, Meta-PS cleverly chains binomial concentration twice to condition on both calibration and adaptation randomness. Although this builds directly on existing PAC prediction set theory, that precise meta-PAC formulation appears new and could interest researchers in safety-critical few-shot applications.  
• Theoretical rigor: The (ε,α,δ)-meta-PAC definition is precisely stated, and the proofs are sound. Related work on conformal and meta-conformal prediction is comprehensive, and the paper clearly differentiates Meta-PS from prior approaches (e.g., Adam Fisch et al.).  
• Broad evaluation: Experiments span vision, language, and medical domains, with appropriate baselines and a variety of meta-learning backbones. Meta-PS reliably meets coverage guarantees and often yields tighter sets, demonstrating practical benefits.  
• Practical simplicity: The algorithm layers neatly on existing PAC set procedures and requires minimal code changes. In contrast to recalibrating at test time, Meta-PS reuses thresholds, which practitioners may find useful for fast adaptation.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:  
1. Interpretability and Usability:  
   – The triply-nested guarantee (conditioning on calibration tasks, adaptation sets, then test examples) is hard to parse. Users may struggle to choose ε, α, and δ in practice. I recommend adding a brief user guide or illustrative example showing how to set these parameters under typical sample budgets.  
   – A concrete analysis of sample complexity—how N (number of calibration tasks) and per-task sample size n trade off with ε, α, δ—would greatly aid practitioners. Even a table or plot of required N, n for common (ε,α,δ) targets would be helpful.  

2. Clarity of Presentation:  
   – The writing remains dense, particularly Section 4.1 and Figure 2. The orientations of stripes, color coding, and “N” boxes in the plots are not fully explained. I suggest streamlining notation (e.g., unify symbols for score functions and thresholds) and including pseudocode in the main text for the two calibration stages.  
   – Several figures reference undefined symbols; adding clear captions and callouts will help readers follow the conditioning structure. A simplified schematic of the three-parameter guarantee could replace overly busy diagrams.  

3. Assumptions and Robustness:  
   – The method assumes tasks are i.i.d. from a common meta-distribution. In real-world settings with covariate or task heterogeneity, this may break. I encourage the authors to include an empirical study (or at least a discussion) of Meta-PS’s sensitivity when calibration and test tasks diverge, and to comment on potential diagnostic checks for assumption violations.  
   – The impact of task non-exchangeability or drift on coverage guarantees should be discussed, perhaps with suggestions on how to adapt thresholds when tasks are non-stationary.

4. Computational Profile and Reproducibility:  
   – The two-stage calibration incurs extra runtime and memory costs compared to single-stage PAC methods, especially for large N. A brief runtime/memory comparison would inform decisions on large-scale deployment.  
   – While the authors indicate resources are available, code release is still pending. To facilitate adoption, please share code, detailed hyperparameter settings, and scripts to reproduce the main experiments.

5. Attribution and Terminology:  
   – The idea of training-conditional prediction sets originates in Vovk [21]. Rebranding them as “PAC prediction sets” without credit to Vovk can mislead readers. I recommend revising all such attributions, crediting Vovk as the inventor of training-conditional sets, and reserving [3] for the deep learning application.  
   – Consistent terminology (e.g., “training-conditional validity” vs. “PAC prediction sets”) will avoid confusion and accurately reflect provenance.

6. Societal and Safety Considerations:  
   – Over-reliance on high-probability coverage guarantees can have real consequences in clinical or safety-critical domains. A more detailed discussion of potential negative impacts—such as misinterpretation of bounds when assumptions fail—and guidelines for safe deployment would strengthen the paper.

Questions for the Authors:  
1. How sensitive is Meta-PS to the choice of N and per-task budget n? Can you provide empirical curves illustrating coverage or set size as these vary?  
2. Have you tested Meta-PS under task distribution shifts or heterogeneity? How do guarantees degrade?  
3. What is the wall-clock runtime (and memory) overhead of two-stage calibration versus single-stage PAC?  
4. Could the threshold aggregation step be extended to handle multidimensional parameters or non-scalar set shapes?  
5. When will the code and detailed reproducibility materials be released?

Decision: 6: Weak Accept  
Despite its dense presentation and some practical gaps (interpretability, complexity analysis, attribution fixes), Meta-PS offers a novel, theoretically grounded, and empirically validated extension of PAC prediction sets to meta-learning. Its potential impact on safety-critical few-shot tasks, combined with solid proofs and comprehensive experiments, justify a weak accept, contingent on addressing the clarity, usability, and attribution concerns outlined above.

reviews.summary: The paper proposed a method for estimating the prediction sets in the meta-test stage from the estimated prediction sets gathered from the meta-train adaptation and calibration phases. The whole process is carefully designed to satisfy the PAC setting in provide a theoretical guarantee for generating the meta-test prediction sets without calibration. The proposed method is tested on four different datasets across three application domains with competitive results.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The proposed method is guaranteed by PAC bound. 

Weaknesses:
1. The paper is not presented in a good way. The notation is very confusing and misleading in general.
2. A big picture is missing. For example, we know that f is a pre-trained meta-model. But in Figure 1 this information is missing which may confuse the meta-learning people.
reviews.questions:
1. In terms of evaluation, I think both predictions set error and prediction set size are considered as the measurements. Among the experiment settings, Meta-PS shows relatively low set error but in some cases set size is larger than others. I think the idea case is that both prediction set error and prediction set size are small. So can the author draw a clear conclusion for the comparison between Meta-CP and Meta-PS?
2. The authors select PS-Binom to estimate \tau. Do different estimators make difference in the performance? 
3. The score functions in lines 108 and 106 have different input spaces. Can the author explain why? My understanding is that f is a meta-learned off-the-shelf model.
reviews.limitations:
1. The paper is not self-contain, the readers who are not familiar with [1] will have a hard time understanding this paper. I will highly suggest the authors to spent some paragraphs explaining some background. 
2. Only one meta-model, prototypical network, is applied as a score function to evaluate the proposed method. 
3. The notations are very confusing, especially those applied for the score function, and prediction set. 
[1] Park, Sangdon, et al. "PAC confidence sets for deep neural networks via calibrated prediction." arXiv preprint arXiv:2001.00106 (2019).
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 2: You are willing to defend your assessment, but it is quite likely that you did not understand the central parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper proposed a method for estimating the prediction sets in the meta-test stage from the estimated prediction sets gathered from the meta-train adaptation and calibration phases. The whole process is carefully designed to satisfy the PAC setting in provide a theoretical guarantee for generating the meta-test prediction sets without calibration. The proposed method is tested on four different datasets across three application domains with competitive results.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The proposed method is guaranteed by PAC bound. 

Weaknesses:
1. The paper is not presented in a good way. The notation is very confusing and misleading in general.
2. A big picture is missing. For example, we know that f is a pre-trained meta-model. But in Figure 1 this information is missing which may confuse the meta-learning people.

Questions:
1. In terms of evaluation, I think both predictions set error and prediction set size are considered as the measurements. Among the experiment settings, Meta-PS shows relatively low set error but in some cases set size is larger than others. I think the idea case is that both prediction set error and prediction set size are small. So can the author draw a clear conclusion for the comparison between Meta-CP and Meta-PS?
2. The authors select PS-Binom to estimate \tau. Do different estimators make difference in the performance? 
3. The score functions in lines 108 and 106 have different input spaces. Can the author explain why? My understanding is that f is a meta-learned off-the-shelf model.

Limitations:
1. The paper is not self-contain, the readers who are not familiar with [1] will have a hard time understanding this paper. I will highly suggest the authors to spent some paragraphs explaining some background. 
2. Only one meta-model, prototypical network, is applied as a score function to evaluate the proposed method. 
3. The notations are very confusing, especially those applied for the score function, and prediction set. 
[1] Park, Sangdon, et al. "PAC confidence sets for deep neural networks via calibrated prediction." arXiv preprint arXiv:2001.00106 (2019).

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

title: Grounded Reinforcement Learning: Learning to Win the Game under Human Commands

reviews.summary:
Paper introduces a new task called “Grounded Reinforcement Learning” in which agents must be reward-maximizing with respect to some high-level language “constraint” (given by another agent/human). The authors demonstrate that existing vanilla RL, constrained RL (CRL), and behavior cloning (BC) fail to perform well both with and without commands in the MiniRTS environment (an example of this problem setup).

To solve this problem setting, the authors propose a new method RED - Reinforced Demonstration Distillation, which repeatedly alternates between pure RL and BC updates.  The authors demonstrate that RED is both the strongest and most grounded policy. The authors provide, through ablations, compelling arguments.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

Introduces (somewhat) of a new problem statement

Clearly written 

Human Evaluation is compelling

Impactful work from a safety perspective. 

Weaknesses:

My largest criticism is the use of the word "language" and i think this undermines the entire approach - the current representation treats commands as one-hot representations. These are not learnt, shaped or any different to the state space. This has no standard components which i would consider natural language, such as pragmatics, syntax and semantics.

RED algorithm does not seem novel (effectively mixture of two)

Results are not significantly better than baselines.
reviews.questions:
1) It is unclear how this approach is different from having standard RL with a combined state space over both command and game state.  As the command representation is not learnt - it seems unclear how this actually is different to a standard RL problem.

2) Can more justification be given to how difficult the opponent you play with is? Can you play with a harder agent to more clearly demonstrate the advantages of your method?

3) It is really unclear how the command token is meaningfully different to having an additional dimension within the game state. Can some justification be given to how either this representation is learnt or has any assemblance of language (is it compositional, have semantic or pragmatic properties).
reviews.limitations:
Addressing the above questions would be useful. 

I also believe actually using a language model would take great steps in demonstrating the effectiveness of your approach.
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper formulates Grounded Reinforcement Learning (GRL), where an agent must both maximize task performance (winning a real‐time strategy game) and obey high‐level, potentially sub‐optimal human commands. The authors cast GRL as a constrained RL problem, approximating obedience via a behavior cloning (BC) loss, and introduce REinforced Demonstration Distillation (RED), an iterative algorithm alternating between pure RL updates on an unconditioned “no‐command” policy and BC updates on a mixture of human demonstrations and self‐generated winning trajectories. Experiments in the MiniRTS environment—including win‐rate comparisons under oracle, random, and adversarial commands, extensive ablations, and a human‐subject study—show that RED outperforms pure RL, BC, joint optimization, soft‐constraint, and IRL baselines in both task success and command fidelity. Code and datasets are released.

Strengths:
1. Novel Task Formulation: The GRL setup, which demands obedience under sub‐optimal instructions, addresses an important gap between language grounding and safe, human‐compatible RL.  
2. Empirical Breadth: The paper includes comprehensive baselines (pure RL, IRL, joint RL/BC, switching), ablations on command noise, data ratios (D_k∶D), and command types, as well as out‐of‐distribution evaluations.  
3. Human Evaluation: A user study confirms that RED strikes a superior balance between following commands and winning performance, lending practical validation beyond simulation metrics.  
4. Clarity and Reproducibility: The manuscript is generally well organized, with clear algorithmic descriptions and extensive supplementary material. The public release of code and data further strengthens reproducibility.  
5. Safety and Impact: By examining obedience to sub‐optimal commands, this work has clear implications for developing assistive agents in safety‐critical settings.

Weaknesses:
1. Command Representation and “Language” Framing: As noted in the main review, commands are encoded as one‐hot tokens fed to an LSTM encoder, without learned semantics, compositionality, or syntactic structure. This undermines the claim of “language” grounding and raises the question of whether the setup simply augments the state space with discrete features.  
2. Limited Theoretical Justification: Both the constrained RL formulation and the iterative distillation regime lack formal analysis of convergence or constraint satisfaction. The choice of the BC‐loss threshold (δ) and the alternation schedule between RL and BC are heuristic, and sensitivity analyses are limited.  
3. Restricted Domain and Vocabulary: All experiments are confined to MiniRTS with a fixed, small command set. It remains unclear how RED generalizes to richer, compositional, or temporally extended language instructions, or to continuous‐action or partially observable tasks.  
4. Modest Novelty of RED: Conceptually, RED alternates between unconstrained policy optimization and behavior cloning—a combination of ideas familiar from self‐distillation and RL with demonstrations. The paper would benefit from deeper discussion of how RED differs from multi‐task or curriculum‐learning approaches that also interleave RL and imitation.  
5. Evaluation Gaps and Hyperparameter Sensitivity: Key design choices—constraint threshold δ, ratio of self‐play to human data (D_k∶D), LSTM vs. Transformer encoders—are only briefly touched on. The impact of these hyperparameters, as well as computational cost and sample efficiency, require more thorough exploration.

Questions and Suggestions:
1. Constraint Tuning: How is δ selected in practice? Can you provide a sensitivity study showing RED’s performance across a range of thresholds, and possibly an automated schedule for adjusting δ?  
2. Ratio of Demonstrations: The construction of D_k (mixing human and self‐generated trajectories) influences both obedience and performance. Could you show how varying the D_k∶D ratio affects convergence stability and sample efficiency?  
3. Richer Language Encoders: Have you considered replacing the one‐hot + LSTM pipeline with a pretrained Transformer or embedding layer to handle compositional commands, synonyms, or noisy input? How might this affect OOD robustness?  
4. Theoretical Guarantees: Even an informal discussion of convergence properties or constraint‐satisfaction bounds would strengthen the contribution. Are there settings where alternating RL and BC provably reduces a surrogate command‐fidelity loss?  
5. Broader Domains: Do you have preliminary results or plans to evaluate RED in continuous control, navigation with natural language, or robotic manipulation tasks? This would clarify generality beyond MiniRTS.  
6. Negative and Societal Impacts: While obedience is critical in assistive settings, strictly following flawed commands can be harmful. A deeper discussion on safeguarding against malicious or biased instructions (e.g., through human overrides or learned constraint checks) would be valuable.  
7. Human Study Details: Please clarify participant awareness of command quality, statistical significance of preference rankings, and whether there was any first‐task or order bias in your human evaluation.

Overall, this paper presents a timely and technically solid contribution to grounded RL by introducing the GRL problem and the RED algorithm. Despite limitations in theoretical analysis, command representation, and domain scope, the strong empirical results, thorough ablations, and human study justify an acceptance. I recommend acceptance (rating: 7).

reviews.summary:
This work focuses on grounded reinforcement learning. Generally, it means finding a conditional RL policy that can perfectly response to high-level command. 

In this paper, the authors proposed a constraned RL algorithm RED to (1) train an agent that is capable of winning strategy games and (2) follow most human instructions. Concretely, RED uses RL to learn explorative policy while uses BC to imitate human demonstration.

Experiment results suggest RED achieves command-following policies and higher winning rate.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
### Strengths

* The idea is concise. Use BC to learn human behavior while use RL to explore better solution.
* Visualization and videos provided are mostly clear and contain necessary information.
* Fig1 is illustrative and helpful to explain what is grounded reinforcement learning. (It would be better if Fig 1 has no background color and in PDF format)
* Code is released.

### Weaknesses

* This work assumes reward from interactive environment. (limitation)
* The code has no detailed guidance and documentation.
* Though I like Figure 2 and other experiments, the main experiment results in Table 1 is confusing and non-convincing.
* The idea is not novel. Conditional imitation learning is a long lasting topics. Refer to "End-to-end Driving via Conditional Imitation Learning"
* Many details are missing or confusing, such as Table 1 and Lagrangian method.
reviews.questions:
### Questions on method


Sec 4.3 says this work uses Lagrangian multiplier to solve the constrained optimization problem in Eq2. It turns out that, in my understanding, this is simply a weighted loss. A formal Lagrangian method should contain update process of the $\beta$, which is just ignored by this paper (or I miss, please point out). The later "iterative solution" also breaks the so called "Lagrangian method". 

---

Line 240 and Appendix A.2: Could you discuss more details on how you deal with action space? What does the "0/1 action for each unit" means? Who does the selection?

---

How to insert a NA command? IIUC, there exist a fixed set of possible commands right? So NA command should be just one more command in that set. You create embedding for each command in the set and than pass the embedding to the neural network.

---





### Questions on experiments

I can't understand table 1. IIUC, these four baselines have the policies that do not take command as input. Why they have different performance in Table 1 when applying different test commands? Please describe how you turn them as conditional policies. 

For example, since RL is trained without any objective to conduct distinguishable behaviors under different commands, why inserting different test commands will lead to different behavior?

---

I don't think Table 1 is convincing, especially when taking STD into account. 

Line 288: "Both RED and Joint policies substantially outperform Switch (BC) policy with sub-optimal commands (Random and Human Proxy), suggesting the conditioned behaviors are improved." This is a overclaimed statement. I can't draw this conclusion from Table 1.

---

I like Fig 2, which shows the dependency between commands and behaviors. Many "conditional RL" methods fail to prove that the conditional policy has casuality to the input command. This figure also shows that RED has much better "casuality" than RL.

My problem is that, in Figure 2, RL in 0% random commands achieves ~57% win rate. Does this value align with the RL performance with NA test command in Table 1?

Again, if RL is trained compelety without human command, then (1) where you can insert the command to the policy? and (2) why RL performance changes with the percentage of random commands?

---


Figure 6 says RL policy achieves similar win rate compared to RED. IIUC, RL policies are not conditioned on any human command. This result suggest that a human subject cooperates with RED trained conditional policy will lead to RL-comparable performance? This seems to be a violation to Table 1 "Oracle" result, which might lead to 

---

There is only one real gameplay footage provided (less than 2 minutes), with only 6 commands given, which is far less than convincing. Meanwhile, the commands seemed to be programmed/copy-pasted (I did not see any input lag), is there any real human intervention involved in recoding this video?

---

This game seems to be very complicated. Does your first intention of the application of this algorithm to be this high-level game? I understand the miniRTS environment is the only one that fitted the algorithm's need, but if you have alternatives (even if the performance is weak), would you also like to present them in the paper? Having diversity is a huge plus to your paper.








### Questions on paper




### Other questions or concerns



The [google drive link](https://drive.google.com/file/d/129ZV9CU6zUnp5B4v0p91Xaae8-M5zxHy/view) provided is **not anonymous**. This is a violation of the NeurIPS Code of Conduct (double-blind reviewing policy). Please action ASAP.

---

This paper has experiments involving 30 college students experiments, are attendees aware of their rights and responsibilities? A more explicit Ethics Statement should be included.

---

Conditional BC is not a new idea. Please refer to this work: End-to-end Driving via Conditional Imitation Learning

---

Appendix D, as said in the checklist, has no information on human experiment. You moved them to Appendix E.

---

The website given in the appendix is very informative and I like the real-time gameplay footage, but there are several problems I want to point out:
- Code link is broken.
- Many grammar errors, will not be present here (plural or singular, tense, etc)
reviews.limitations:
The authors provide limitation section.

GRL is an interesting field. How to improve human-AI cooperation in test-time, how to learn a better human proxy, and how to further encourage, illustrate, quantify the causity between command and behaviors might be future directions.
