title: Understanding Robust Learning through the Lens of Representation Similarities

reviews.summary:
The authors examine the effects of adversarial robustness training on representation. Specifically, they use CKA to compare robust vs non-robust networks, benign vs. adversarial inputs, and how these comparisons change over the course of learning. They find the following results:
- Robust representations are less specialized; distant layers are more similar in robust networks, and block structure is weaker
- Early layers in robust networks are largely unaffected by adversarial examples; representations are similar for benign vs. perturbed inputs
- Deeper layers overfit during robust learning
- Models trained to be robust to different threat models have similar representations
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths: The paper follows in a well-established tradition of using representational similarity analysis to understand neural network behavior and training interventions. The analyses are well-motivated and straightforward. The results are generally presented clearly and easy to understand. The results are interesting.

Weaknesses: The paper seems somewhat limited in scope: It primarily addresses adversarial (worst-case) robustness, which is only one type of robustness. I'm not totally convinced of the utility of this work; it could at the very least do a better job situating itself within existing robustness research. It's unclear if any of the experiments were run in replicate. The figures captions could be more informative and self-contained.

Overall, I think this work could be suitable for publication if it is sufficiently revised.
reviews.questions:
This work primarily examines adversarial (i.e. worst-case) robustness, which is one of many types of robustness, among others including average-case robustness (Hendrycks and Dietterich, Benchmarking Neural Network Robustness to Common Corruptions and Perturbations) and natural adversarial examples (Hendrycks et al.). It’s not clear how important of a problem adversarial robustness is (see Gilmer et al.’s Motivating the Rules of the Game for Adversarial Example Research). The authors examine robustness to Gabor, snow, and jpeg attacks, but the analysis and results are limited. I would encourage the researchers to more systematically examine how their findings extend to other types of robustness.

Are the results from a single instance of each model? If so, I would strongly encourage the authors to repeat their analyses in triplicate at a minimum. If this is computationally cost-prohibitive, then perhaps they could focus on the most important results.

There is relevant previous work examining the relationship between representational dimensionality and adversarial robustness (Leavitt and Morcos, Linking average- and worst-case perturbation robustness via class selectivity and dimensionality; Sanyal et al., Robustness vai Deep Low-rank Representations; Nayebi and Ganguli, Biologically inspired protection of deep networks from adversarial attacks). These papers show that low rank representations confer adversarial robustness. Can you reconcile these results with your findings that adversarial robustness is associated with greater utilization of model capacity? Additionally, most of these works (certainly Leavitt and Morcos and Sanyal et al.) regularized representational dimensionality and found that it improved robustness; it would be quite interesting to investigate whether that the inverse holds: does adversarial training reduce representational dimensionality?

The claim that “Deeper layers overfit during robust learning” seems like it could be leveraged to improve the generalization gap caused by robust learning. The simplest approach would be to simply stop training when the adversarial loss begins to rise (as in Figure 6). I assume this has drawbacks, but the authors don’t present the data (e.g. accuracy curves over training). Is this overfitting a necessary component of robust learning?

Lines 59-61: “On the other hand, the representations of benign and perturbed inputs from robust networks are indistinguishable from one another with regards to representation similarity metrics.” I think to make this claim the authors need to directly compare CKA(Benign, Perturbed) to CKA(Benign, Benign) and CKA(Perturbed, Perturbed).

The caption for Figure 2 should describe the model architecture(s) used to generate the results, as well as whether the data are benign or perturbed. There are other Figures which lack important experimental details, such as whether the data are benign or perturbed.

Comparing the CKA results in Figure 2d to the results in Figures 2a-c make it clear that the similarity between robust and non-robust networks is lower than the similarity between networks of the same type, but I think it’s important to have a negative baseline—how low is “low”? Accordingly, I think the authors should repeat the analysis (CKA between robust and non-robust networks) using sample-shuffled data (and/or some other suitable random baseline).

Showing that the block structure effect varies with the strength of adversarial training is a nice experiment and result.

The results presented in lines 174-188 (“Impact of robust training strength” and “Impact of architecture”) would be easier to interpret (and their motivation clearer) if you introduced them with Nguyen et al.’s finding that “block structure in the internal representations arises in models that are heavily overparameterized relative to the training dataset.” While you do cite their work (“Previously Nguyen et al. [28] observed that increasing network width, thus capacity, leads to emergence of block-structure in non-robust networks”), I think this is an insufficient description of their results and should be presented earlier.

“Overfitting is predominantly visible in later layers”: I would suggest a follow-up experiment to more thoroughly test this claim: freeze early layers (blocks 1 and/or 2) early in training. If early layers aren’t involved in the overfitting and training instability, then the freezing should have minimal effect on these phenomena.

Lines 278-280: “When using CKA to compare against the Snow threat model, we observe that the highest average similarity is achieved with Gabor. This represents a novel insight into these threat classes, as correspondence between the two was not previously known.” This result seems very specific and not particularly useful. I would encourage the authors to find a more general result (see my earlier comment about examining different attack types).

minor comments:

Lines 21-22: “...such as images, speech or text”. It’s a matter of personal taste, but I am a proponent of the Oxford comma: “...such as images, speech, or text”

Line 22: What does “meaningful” mean in this context? Meaningful with regards to what?

Line 187: “These results suggest that while increasing width in robust networks doesn’t lead a drastic shift in similarity of internal layer representations.” Typo?

Typo in caption of Figure 5 (...to understand similairty)
reviews.limitations: The authors devote a paragraph at the end of the discussion to the limitations of their study, which seems appropriate given the space limitations. I do, however, think the authors could be more careful and nuanced about some of their claims.
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

[28] observed that increasing network width, thus capacity, leads to emergence of block-structure in non-robust networks”), I think this is an insufficient description of their results and should be presented earlier.

I would encourage the authors to find a more general result (see my earlier comment about examining different attack types).

Lines 21-22: “...such as images, speech or text”. It’s a matter of personal taste, but I am a proponent of the Oxford comma: “...such as images, speech, or text”

Limitations:

reviews.text:
Summary:  
This paper presents a systematic study of how adversarially robust training reshapes the internal representations of deep neural networks. Using Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA), the authors compare layer-wise activations of robust versus standard models across CIFAR-10 and two ImageNet subsets (ImageNette and ImageWoof) with Wide ResNets and ResNet-50. They report four key findings:  
1. Robust networks exhibit higher long-range similarity and a loss of the familiar “block structure” and specialization seen in standard networks.  
2. Early layers remain largely unaffected by adversarial perturbations in both robust and non-robust models, whereas deeper layers diverge sharply and tend to overfit during robust training.  
3. Models trained against different threat types (e.g., PGD with ℓ∞ budgets, JPEG, Gabor, Snow) yield highly similar internal representations at low budgets, suggesting latent correspondences among diverse attacks.  
4. Increasing robust training strength and network width modulates—but does not eliminate—the observed phenomena (block-structure disappearance, deeper-layer overfitting).  

Strengths:  
• Novel application of representation similarity analysis to adversarial robustness. Building on prior CKA work, the authors deliver new empirical insights—loss of layer specialization, persistent early-layer stability, and cross-threat representational alignment—that go beyond existing dimensionality-robustness studies.  
• Thorough experimentation. The paper examines multiple datasets, architectures (Wide ResNets and ResNet-50), threat models, and budgets. Ablations on width, budget, and architecture reinforce the generality of the findings.  
• Clear presentation and reproducibility. Figures illustrating cross-layer heatmaps and CKA trajectories over training are effective, and the code is made publicly available. The layer-wise analyses are motivated by both theoretical questions and practical diagnostics for robust training methods.  

Weaknesses and Suggestions for Revision:  
1. Scope of robustness. The study focuses exclusively on worst-case adversarial robustness (PGD/TRADES). It would strengthen the work to situate it within broader robustness paradigms, such as common corruptions (average-case robustness) and natural adversarial examples. Even a small set of experiments on standard corruptions (e.g., Hendrycks and Dietterich’s benchmarks) could contextualize whether the block-structure and overfitting phenomena generalize beyond adversarial settings.  
2. Single-run analyses and statistical rigor. It is unclear whether the reported results derive from single training runs. To assess variability, the authors should repeat key experiments in triplicate (or provide confidence intervals/error bars for CKA scores and accuracy curves). Reporting variance would bolster confidence in the stability of long-range similarity and overfitting observations.  
3. Exclusive reliance on CKA. While CKA is a powerful metric, relying solely on it may overlook other aspects of representation geometry. A comparison against alternative similarity measures (e.g., PWCCA, Procrustes distance) would help confirm that the observed phenomena are not CKA artifacts.  
4. Causal interpretation and actionable remedies. The paper hypothesizes that deeper-layer overfitting underlies robust generalization gaps and suggests layer freezing or early stopping. However, no experiments validate these interventions. Incorporating a pilot study—e.g., freezing early blocks or halting training when adversarial loss rises—would transform correlative insights into practical guidance.  
5. Baseline and negative controls. Introducing a random baseline, such as computing CKA on sample-shuffled activations, would contextualize “low” similarity values between robust and non-robust models. This negative control would clarify the statistical significance of cross-model differences.  
6. Figure details and clarity. Some figure captions lack critical information (model architectures, whether inputs are benign or perturbed). Improving captions to be self-contained and ensuring axis labels are legible will enhance readability. Minor typos (e.g., “similiarity”) and inconsistent epsilon normalization (raw vs. scaled) should be corrected.  
7. Computational and societal considerations. A brief discussion of the environmental cost of robust training (longer convergence times, multiple runs) and potential ethical implications—such as how robust models might be leveraged for stronger transferable attacks—would round out the limitations.

Questions for the Authors:  
1. Have you evaluated whether the core findings hold on full-scale datasets (e.g., full ImageNet) or non-vision modalities (text, speech)?  
2. How sensitive are your results to the choice of similarity metric (CKA vs. PWCCA or Procrustes)?  
3. Can you provide variance estimates or confidence intervals for CKA scores across multiple runs?  
4. Did you experiment with early stopping or freezing of early layers to mitigate deeper-layer overfitting, and if so, how did these interventions affect robust accuracy and clean accuracy?  
5. How were CKA centering and batching parameters chosen, and do these choices materially impact your conclusions?  
6. Could you quantify the relationship between loss of specialization (block-structure disappearance) and downstream task performance beyond classification accuracy?  

Rating: 7: Accept  
This work is technically sound and offers high-impact diagnostic insights into adversarially robust training. While the paper would benefit from additional controls, statistical rigor, and preliminary causal interventions, the core contributions are clear and of significant interest to the robustness and representation learning communities. With the suggested revisions, this study will serve as a valuable reference for both theoretical analyses and practical improvements in robust model design.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The authors examine the effects of adversarial robustness training on representation. Specifically, they use CKA to compare robust vs non-robust networks, benign vs. adversarial inputs, and how these comparisons change over the course of learning. They find the following results:
- Robust representations are less specialized; distant layers are more similar in robust networks, and block structure is weaker
- Early layers in robust networks are largely unaffected by adversarial examples; representations are similar for benign vs. perturbed inputs
- Deeper layers overfit during robust learning
- Models trained to be robust to different threat models have similar representations

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths: The paper follows in a well-established tradition of using representational similarity analysis to understand neural network behavior and training interventions. The analyses are well-motivated and straightforward. The results are generally presented clearly and easy to understand. The results are interesting.

Weaknesses: The paper seems somewhat limited in scope: It primarily addresses adversarial (worst-case) robustness, which is only one type of robustness. I'm not totally convinced of the utility of this work; it could at the very least do a better job situating itself within existing robustness research. It's unclear if any of the experiments were run in replicate. The figures captions could be more informative and self-contained.

Overall, I think this work could be suitable for publication if it is sufficiently revised.

Questions:
This work primarily examines adversarial (i.e. worst-case) robustness, which is one of many types of robustness, among others including average-case robustness (Hendrycks and Dietterich, Benchmarking Neural Network Robustness to Common Corruptions and Perturbations) and natural adversarial examples (Hendrycks et al.). It’s not clear how important of a problem adversarial robustness is (see Gilmer et al.’s Motivating the Rules of the Game for Adversarial Example Research). The authors examine robustness to Gabor, snow, and jpeg attacks, but the analysis and results are limited. I would encourage the researchers to more systematically examine how their findings extend to other types of robustness.

Are the results from a single instance of each model? If so, I would strongly encourage the authors to repeat their analyses in triplicate at a minimum. If this is computationally cost-prohibitive, then perhaps they could focus on the most important results.

There is relevant previous work examining the relationship between representational dimensionality and adversarial robustness (Leavitt and Morcos, Linking average- and worst-case perturbation robustness via class selectivity and dimensionality; Sanyal et al., Robustness vai Deep Low-rank Representations; Nayebi and Ganguli, Biologically inspired protection of deep networks from adversarial attacks). These papers show that low rank representations confer adversarial robustness. Can you reconcile these results with your findings that adversarial robustness is associated with greater utilization of model capacity? Additionally, most of these works (certainly Leavitt and Morcos and Sanyal et al.) regularized representational dimensionality and found that it improved robustness; it would be quite interesting to investigate whether that the inverse holds: does adversarial training reduce representational dimensionality?

The claim that “Deeper layers overfit during robust learning” seems like it could be leveraged to improve the generalization gap caused by robust learning. The simplest approach would be to simply stop training when the adversarial loss begins to rise (as in Figure 6). I assume this has drawbacks, but the authors don’t present the data (e.g. accuracy curves over training). Is this overfitting a necessary component of robust learning?

Lines 59-61: “On the other hand, the representations of benign and perturbed inputs from robust networks are indistinguishable from one another with regards to representation similarity metrics.” I think to make this claim the authors need to directly compare CKA(Benign, Perturbed) to CKA(Benign, Benign) and CKA(Perturbed, Perturbed).

The caption for Figure 2 should describe the model architecture(s) used to generate the results, as well as whether the data are benign or perturbed. There are other Figures which lack important experimental details, such as whether the data are benign or perturbed.

Comparing the CKA results in Figure 2d to the results in Figures 2a-c make it clear that the similarity between robust and non-robust networks is lower than the similarity between networks of the same type, but I think it’s important to have a negative baseline—how low is “low”? Accordingly, I think the authors should repeat the analysis (CKA between robust and non-robust networks) using sample-shuffled data (and/or some other suitable random baseline).

Showing that the block structure effect varies with the strength of adversarial training is a nice experiment and result.

The results presented in lines 174-188 (“Impact of robust training strength” and “Impact of architecture”) would be easier to interpret (and their motivation clearer) if you introduced them with Nguyen et al.’s finding that “block structure in the internal representations arises in models that are heavily overparameterized relative to the training dataset.” While you do cite their work (“Previously Nguyen et al. [28] observed that increasing network width, thus capacity, leads to emergence of block-structure in non-robust networks”), I think this is an insufficient description of their results and should be presented earlier.

“Overfitting is predominantly visible in later layers”: I would suggest a follow-up experiment to more thoroughly test this claim: freeze early layers (blocks 1 and/or 2) early in training. If early layers aren’t involved in the overfitting and training instability, then the freezing should have minimal effect on these phenomena.

Lines 278-280: “When using CKA to compare against the Snow threat model, we observe that the highest average similarity is achieved with Gabor. This represents a novel insight into these threat classes, as correspondence between the two was not previously known.” This result seems very specific and not particularly useful. I would encourage the authors to find a more general result (see my earlier comment about examining different attack types).

minor comments:

Lines 21-22: “...such as images, speech or text”. It’s a matter of personal taste, but I am a proponent of the Oxford comma: “...such as images, speech, or text”

Line 22: What does “meaningful” mean in this context? Meaningful with regards to what?

Line 187: “These results suggest that while increasing width in robust networks doesn’t lead a drastic shift in similarity of internal layer representations.” Typo?

Typo in caption of Figure 5 (...to understand similairty)

Limitations:
The authors devote a paragraph at the end of the discussion to the limitations of their study, which seems appropriate given the space limitations. I do, however, think the authors could be more careful and nuanced about some of their claims.

Rating:
7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.original_rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.

reviews.summary: This paper uses an existing method for comparisons of intermediate neural network activations (CKA) for a comparison of robust and non-robust networks. The authors analyze the similarities in different aspects and try to deduce insights in adversarial training.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
- The paper uses an interesting & potentially insightful approach to better understand how adversarially trained networks process information and how they differ from non-robust networks.
- The attached code for this submission is well documented and looks clean, making the results more trustworthy.
- It feels like the paper spends too much time describing plots/results compared to interpreting the results and presenting hypotheses for what results mean.
reviews.questions:
- Missing related work: There has been previous work on comparing the features of robust vs. non-robust neural networks that should be cited, e.g. using feature visualizations [1].
- L25: Reference missing.
- L45f: Reference [18] should be placed behind Imagewoof and not behind Imagenet; also, a reference for ImageNet is missing. 
- L93: E.g., JPEG compression doesn't seem like an additive perturbation.
- L108: If this is well known, I encourage the authors to support this sentence with (multiple) references.
- L118: A more detailed critical discussion of the possible shortcomings of these metrics and why they don't impact the results of this paper would be good.
- L123f: Why doesn't this impact your results? This definitely needs to be supported by a strong argument. 
- Figure 2/3: On which datasets were these metrics calculated? On the vanilla test data or on adversarially perturbed versions of the test data? What does the figure look like for the other data type (either benign or adversarial)?
- Figure 2/3: Given that the clean accuracy goes down for adversarial training, I'm wondering whether the change in the similarity plots is really due to a special aspect of adversarial training or just due to the drop in performance, i.e. how do these plots look like for non-robust networks that are not trained until convergence but until they reach a similar test accuracy as the robust networks?
- L163f: That seems a bit like an overstatement: The block structure is still clearly visible for 2 out of the 3 datasets - it just gets weaker.
- Multiple typos/grammar mistakes that make it sometimes break the flow of the text, e.g. L172, L187, L201, L216, L221, L235.
- Figure 4: These plots should be larger - the text is hard to read.
- Figure 4: The first sentence of the caption doesn't read right/is difficult to parse.
- L185f: Did the authors observe the same behavior as described by Nguyen et al. when they tried to reproduce their observations for non-robust networks? At the moment it is difficult to confidently say that the observation reported here is because of the robust network or because of the experimental setup used by the authors.
- L213: If this is well known, I again encourage the authors to support this sentence with (multiple) references. Furthermore, this is actually not such a clear property, and there are specific attacks that just aim to create adversarial examples that transfer well between different models.
- Minor comment: The paper uses inconsistent notation - sometimes the authors say "Figure", sometimes "figure"; sometimes "Appendix", sometimes "App.".
- L252f: Where can we see both validation and training loss? The figure only shows one "loss" but doesn't say which one it is.
- L265: I wouldn't call these "adversarial perturbations" but rather use the more commonly used expression common corruptions. Especially, since at least for JPEG compression there is nothing you can optimize - so that doesn't really fit the overall adversarial framework.
- Section 6: What are the specific parameters of the JPEG, snow, and Gabor corruptions?
- L289: What is the conclusion/interpretation of the results?

[1] Leveraging Sparse Linear Layers for Debuggable Deep Networks. Eric Wong, Shibani Santurkar, Aleksander Mądry. 2021
reviews.limitations:
- While the authors mentioned criticism on the similarity metric they use (CKA), they don't really explain why this doesn't apply to their analysis. This should be properly addressed.
- It is also unclear whether the results differ if a different adversarial attack (with the same thread model) was used for generating the adversarial perturbations - this should ideally also be addressed.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper uses an existing method for comparisons of intermediate neural network activations (CKA) for a comparison of robust and non-robust networks. The authors analyze the similarities in different aspects and try to deduce insights in adversarial training.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
- The paper uses an interesting & potentially insightful approach to better understand how adversarially trained networks process information and how they differ from non-robust networks.
- The attached code for this submission is well documented and looks clean, making the results more trustworthy.
- It feels like the paper spends too much time describing plots/results compared to interpreting the results and presenting hypotheses for what results mean.

Questions:
- Missing related work: There has been previous work on comparing the features of robust vs. non-robust neural networks that should be cited, e.g. using feature visualizations [1].
- L25: Reference missing.
- L45f: Reference [18] should be placed behind Imagewoof and not behind Imagenet; also, a reference for ImageNet is missing. 
- L93: E.g., JPEG compression doesn't seem like an additive perturbation.
- L108: If this is well known, I encourage the authors to support this sentence with (multiple) references.
- L118: A more detailed critical discussion of the possible shortcomings of these metrics and why they don't impact the results of this paper would be good.
- L123f: Why doesn't this impact your results? This definitely needs to be supported by a strong argument. 
- Figure 2/3: On which datasets were these metrics calculated? On the vanilla test data or on adversarially perturbed versions of the test data? What does the figure look like for the other data type (either benign or adversarial)?
- Figure 2/3: Given that the clean accuracy goes down for adversarial training, I'm wondering whether the change in the similarity plots is really due to a special aspect of adversarial training or just due to the drop in performance, i.e. how do these plots look like for non-robust networks that are not trained until convergence but until they reach a similar test accuracy as the robust networks?
- L163f: That seems a bit like an overstatement: The block structure is still clearly visible for 2 out of the 3 datasets - it just gets weaker.
- Multiple typos/grammar mistakes that make it sometimes break the flow of the text, e.g. L172, L187, L201, L216, L221, L235.
- Figure 4: These plots should be larger - the text is hard to read.
- Figure 4: The first sentence of the caption doesn't read right/is difficult to parse.
- L185f: Did the authors observe the same behavior as described by Nguyen et al. when they tried to reproduce their observations for non-robust networks? At the moment it is difficult to confidently say that the observation reported here is because of the robust network or because of the experimental setup used by the authors.
- L213: If this is well known, I again encourage the authors to support this sentence with (multiple) references. Furthermore, this is actually not such a clear property, and there are specific attacks that just aim to create adversarial examples that transfer well between different models.
- Minor comment: The paper uses inconsistent notation - sometimes the authors say "Figure", sometimes "figure"; sometimes "Appendix", sometimes "App.".
- L252f: Where can we see both validation and training loss? The figure only shows one "loss" but doesn't say which one it is.
- L265: I wouldn't call these "adversarial perturbations" but rather use the more commonly used expression common corruptions. Especially, since at least for JPEG compression there is nothing you can optimize - so that doesn't really fit the overall adversarial framework.
- Section 6: What are the specific parameters of the JPEG, snow, and Gabor corruptions?
- L289: What is the conclusion/interpretation of the results?

[1] Leveraging Sparse Linear Layers for Debuggable Deep Networks. Eric Wong, Shibani Santurkar, Aleksander Mądry. 2021

Limitations:
- While the authors mentioned criticism on the similarity metric they use (CKA), they don't really explain why this doesn't apply to their analysis. This should be properly addressed.
- It is also unclear whether the results differ if a different adversarial attack (with the same thread model) was used for generating the adversarial perturbations - this should ideally also be addressed.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper investigates how adversarially robust training reshapes the internal representations of deep neural networks by applying Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) to compare robust and non-robust models. Experiments span CIFAR-10 and two ImageNet subsets (ImageNette, ImageWoof) using Wide ResNets and ResNet-50, under various budgets, architectures, and threat models (PGD, TRADES, JPEG, Gabor, Snow). The authors report that robust networks lose the familiar “block structure,” exhibit heightened long-range similarity across layers, and show deeper-layer overfitting during training. They further observe that, at low perturbation budgets, representations learned under visually distinct threat models align closely.

Strengths:
- Novel application of representation similarity analysis (CKA) to dissect robust vs. standard training, revealing phenomena (e.g., disappearance of block structure, cross-threat representational alignment) not previously characterized.
- Thorough experimentation: multiple datasets, architectures, threat types, and budgets; ablation studies on budget and architecture further support key findings.
- Code release with clear documentation enhances reproducibility and trust in the reported results.
- Insights are potentially actionable, motivating strategies such as layer-freezing schedules, layer-wise specialization regularizers, and informed multi-threat training.

Weaknesses & Suggestions:
1. Overemphasis on Descriptive Results:
   - The manuscript dedicates substantial space to plotting and restating observations without deeper interpretation or hypotheses about underlying mechanisms. Integrating higher-level discussion—e.g., linking long-range similarity to loss landscapes or overfitting dynamics—would strengthen impact.
2. Related Work & References:
   - Several prior studies on robust vs. non-robust feature comparisons (e.g., feature visualizations [Wong et al., 2021]) are missing. Citations for ImageNet and its subsets (ImageNette, ImageWoof) should be added.  
   - Sentences claiming “well-known” properties (L108, L213) need supporting references, and the distinction between adversarial perturbations versus common corruptions (JPEG compression, snow, Gabor) should be clarified with appropriate citations.
3. Critical Discussion of CKA:
   - While the authors note CKA’s limitations, they do not argue why these do not impact their conclusions. A more detailed critical discussion is warranted, and validating key patterns with an alternative similarity metric (e.g., PWCCA or Procrustes distance) would rule out CKA-specific artifacts.
4. Statistical Rigor:
   - CKA heatmaps and accuracy curves lack error bars or confidence intervals, leaving the statistical significance of observed differences unclear. Reporting variance across multiple runs and clarifying centering/batching parameters of CKA would improve methodological solidity.
5. Figures and Notation:
   - Figures 2–4 suffer from small text and dense captions; enlarging plots and simplifying captions will aid readability. Notational inconsistencies (e.g., “Figure” vs “figure,” “Appendix” vs “App.”) and typos (L172, L187, etc.) should be systematically corrected.
6. Dataset and Model Scope:
   - Results are confined to small-scale vision datasets and convolutional networks. Discussion of how findings might extend to full ImageNet, vision transformers, or other modalities (text, speech) is currently speculative; empirical evaluation or at least a clearer roadmap for generalization would strengthen the work.
7. Baseline Controls:
   - It remains unclear whether the changes in similarity arise specifically from adversarial training or simply from reduced test accuracy. A control where non-robust networks are early-stopped to match robust-model accuracy would clarify this point.
8. Interpretation of Corruptions:
   - Referring to JPEG, snow, and Gabor as “adversarial perturbations” is misleading; these align better with the common corruptions literature. The paper should specify corruption parameters (Section 6) and frame them appropriately.

Questions for the Authors:
1. Have you evaluated the sensitivity of your findings to alternative similarity metrics (e.g., PWCCA)? Do core observations persist?
2. Can you provide error bars or confidence intervals for CKA scores and accuracy curves to assess statistical significance?
3. How would similarity patterns change if early layers were frozen based on CKA convergence? Have you attempted such interventions to validate the proposed training strategies?
4. On which data (clean vs. adversarially perturbed) were Figures 2–3 computed? Could you include both for completeness?
5. How were corruption parameters for JPEG, Snow, and Gabor chosen, and can you provide these details to enable reproduction?
6. Have you tested whether non-robust networks stopped at lower accuracies display similar block-structure weakening, to control for accuracy-driven effects?
7. Could you clarify the centering and batching procedures used in your online CKA implementation?

Recommendation:
6: Weak Accept. The paper presents technically sound and reproducible analyses that yield valuable diagnostic insights into adversarially robust representations. However, it would benefit from deeper interpretative discussion, stronger citations, statistical rigor, and expanded evaluation (alternative metrics, controls, broader datasets) to fully substantiate its conclusions.

reviews.summary: The paper contrasts representation similarities of networks trained to perform image classification with and without adversarial noise. To do so, the authors measure the similarity of representations using the Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) metric (as well two other similarity metrics in appendix) for CIFAR-10 and two subsets of ImageNet. The authors highlight 1) networks trained with adversarial noise have layers similar to one another compared to those of standard trained networks (which have a block structure) 2) representations in early layers are unaffected by adversarial perturbations both for standard and adversarially trained networks 3) networks trained with and without adversarial perturbations have similar representations until the last 10 or so layers 4) early layers converge faster and later layers overfit to local minima. The authors also analyze the similarity of representations when other perturbations are applied JPEG, Gabor, and Snow, finding
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
This work investigates representations learned for image classification by contrasting the representation similarities of networks trained with and without adversarial noise—leading to several insights into properties of the learned representations as well as learning dynamics of modern networks. Such properties as the authors point out are not captured by aggregate performance metrics such as loss or accuracy, leading to insights about the learned representations. The paper is well-written, experiments clearly described, and motivation is well-grounded.

The experiments conducted are convincing and well-formulated. For example, multiple similarity metrics are compared, variants of the adversarial perturbations are explored, and experimental claims are well-founded. 
However, the authors'claims should be sufficiently couched within the experimental settings studied: supervised image classification for ResNet-based models on CIFAR-10 and two subsets of ImageNet. For example, the work only studies ResNet-based architectures yet claims to cover “DNNs with different architectures” (line 12) and the “impact of choice of architecture” (line 37). I would expect a comparison of “different architectures” to encompass for example transformer-based architectures, MLP-architectures, etc. I suggest the authors more explicitly couch claims in the introduction, abstract, title, and conclusions within the confines of the experimental settings studied. For example 
The comparison of representations for "threat models" (JPEG, Gabor, and Snow) in Section 6 was not particularly informative and seemed removed from the primary findings of the remainder of the paper. 

Robust representations is a broad term. This work studies robustness to random noise in the input. Yet, the authors intermix “robustness to adversarial examples” and often plainly use the term “robust network” to describe a particular type: robustness to noise (defined via L-p bounds on the input) throughout the work. For example, robust in the context of image classification can just as well refer to robustness with respect to rendering method ImageNet-Sketch, artifacts such as blurring (ImageNet-C), adversarial examples (ImageNet-A), or even robustness to natural transformations such as pose (Alcorn et al.). I suggest the authors clarify the wording to only include the specific definitions of robustness studied here.

The insights gained from the authors’ analysis are interesting and well-described. The finding of most value, in my opinion, is that later layers overfit (matching existing work relating overfitting in later layers to spurious correlations [1]). While the analysis sheds light on differences between networks trained with and without adversarial noise as well as their learning dynamics the findings are confined to the L-infinity definition of robustness studied for supervised image classification using ResNet-based architectures (for the main claims in section 4 and 5).

[1] “Last Layer Re-Training is Sufficient for Robustness to Spurious Correlations” [https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.02937](https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.02937)

The paper is well-written, experiments clearly described, and motivation is well-grounded.

The experiments conducted are convincing and well-formulated. For example, multiple similarity metrics are compared, variants of the adversarial perturbations are explored, and experimental claims are well-founded. 
However, the authors'claims should be sufficiently couched within the experimental settings studied: supervised image classification for ResNet-based models on CIFAR-10 and two subsets of ImageNet. For example, the work only studies ResNet-based architectures yet claims to cover “DNNs with different architectures” (line 12) and the “impact of choice of architecture” (line 37). I would expect a comparison of “different architectures” to encompass for example transformer-based architectures, MLP-architectures, etc. I suggest the authors more explicitly couch claims in the introduction, abstract, title, and conclusions within the confines of the experimental settings studied. For example 
The comparison of representations for "threat models" (JPEG, Gabor, and Snow) in Section 6 was not particularly informative and seemed removed from the primary findings of the remainder of the paper. 

Robust representations is a broad term. This work studies robustness to random noise in the input. Yet, the authors intermix “robustness to adversarial examples” and often plainly use the term “robust network” to describe a particular type: robustness to noise (defined via L-p bounds on the input) throughout the work. For example, robust in the context of image classification can just as well refer to robustness with respect to rendering method ImageNet-Sketch, artifacts such as blurring (ImageNet-C), adversarial examples (ImageNet-A), or even robustness to natural transformations such as pose (Alcorn et al.). I suggest the authors clarify the wording to only include the specific definitions of robustness studied here.

The insights gained from the authors’ analysis are interesting and well-described. The finding of most value, in my opinion, is that later layers overfit (matching existing work relating overfitting in later layers to spurious correlations [1]). While the analysis sheds light on differences between networks trained with and without adversarial noise as well as their learning dynamics the findings are confined to the L-infinity definition of robustness studied for supervised image classification using ResNet-based architectures (for the main claims in section 4 and 5).

[1] “Last Layer Re-Training is Sufficient for Robustness to Spurious Correlations” [https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.02937](https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.02937)

Questions:
included above

Limitations:
Yes.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper presents a systematic analysis of how adversarially robust training alters the internal representations of deep neural networks. Using centered kernel alignment (CKA) as the primary similarity metric (with two additional metrics in the appendix), the authors compare layer-wise representations of ResNet-based models trained on CIFAR-10 and two ImageNet subsets (ImageNette, ImageWoof), with and without ℓ∞ adversarial perturbations. They report four key findings: (1) robustly trained networks lose the familiar “block structure” of standard models and exhibit higher long-range similarity across layers; (2) early layers remain largely unaffected by adversarial noise in both robust and non-robust networks; (3) robust and non-robust representations track closely until roughly the last ten layers; and (4) during training, early layers converge quickly while deeper layers tend to overfit local minima. An additional analysis considers other perturbations (JPEG compression, Gabor, Snow), revealing some cross-perturbation representational alignment.

Strengths:
• Novel insights via representation similarity.  By applying CKA to contrast robust and standard training, the work uncovers previously unreported phenomena—loss of layer specialization, heightened long-range similarity, and deeper-layer overfitting—offering diagnostic guidance beyond aggregate accuracy or loss metrics.  
• Thorough experimentation.  The authors evaluate multiple threat models, budgets, and architectures (Wide ResNets, ResNet-50), and include ablations on perturbation types.  Experiments are well described, reproducible (with open-source code), and supported by clear heatmaps and quantitative plots.  
• Well-written and organized.  Background on adversarial robustness and CKA is concise, and each section addresses a distinct research question.  Motivation for understanding representational dynamics is clearly articulated, and the manuscript flows logically from methodology to results.  
• Actionable directions.  The paper suggests concrete strategies—layer freezing schedules, layer-wise regularization, principled threat-model selection—for future work aiming to mitigate clean-accuracy loss and overfitting in robust training.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:
1. Scope of claims.  While the experiments focus exclusively on ResNet-based architectures in supervised image classification, some statements (e.g., covering “DNNs with different architectures” or “impact of choice of architecture”) are broader than the evidence supports.  I recommend explicitly couching such claims—especially in the abstract, introduction, and conclusions—to reflect the settings studied.  
2. Reliance on CKA.  All analyses use CKA, but alternative metrics (e.g., PWCCA, Procrustes distances) could validate that the observed patterns are not CKA artifacts.  Incorporating a second similarity measure, even on a subset of layers, would strengthen confidence in the results.  
3. Statistical significance.  The paper does not report variability or confidence intervals for CKA scores across multiple runs.  Providing error bars or variance estimates would help assess the robustness of the findings.  
4. Limited architectures and datasets.  The study omits non-convolutional models (e.g., vision transformers) and larger benchmarks (full ImageNet).  Clarifying that generalization to other architectures or modalities remains an open question would set appropriate expectations.  
5. Definition of “robustness.”  The term is used broadly to describe ℓp-bounded adversarial robustness as well as robustness to common corruptions (JPEG, Gabor, Snow).  I suggest consistently defining “robust network” only in the context of the specific ℓ∞ threat studied, and clearly separating analyses of other perturbations.  
6. Detached threat-model comparison.  Section 6 on JPEG, Gabor, and Snow is interesting but feels somewhat disconnected from the core adversarial findings.  Either better motivate how these comparisons inform the main narrative or consider omitting them to maintain focus.  
7. Causal interpretation.  The paper speculates that layer specialization loss drives certain performance trade-offs, but the analyses are correlational.  While causal validation may be beyond the current scope, discussing this limitation explicitly—and perhaps pointing to potential interventions (e.g., freezing early layers based on measured convergence)—would improve clarity.

Detailed Comments:
- Line 12/37: Rephrase to acknowledge that only ResNet variants are studied when discussing “different architectures.”  
- Section 3: Briefly justify CKA centering and batch-sampling procedures; report sensitivity to these hyperparameters if possible.  
- Figures: Some axes labels could be enlarged for readability; include error bars on key CKA heatmaps and similarity curves.  
- Related work: The connection to overfitting in later layers (e.g., “Last Layer Re-Training is Sufficient for Robustness to Spurious Correlations”) is noted; consider expanding this discussion to ground the finding in prior theory.  
- Discussion: Strengthen the link between observed representational dynamics and the proposed remedies (layer-wise regularizers, threat-model selection), or clarify that these are suggestions for future work rather than validated methods.

Questions for the Authors:
1. Have you tried computing PWCCA or orthogonal Procrustes distances on a subset of layers to confirm that the disappearance of block structure is not CKA-specific?  
2. Can you provide variance estimates for CKA similarity scores across multiple random seeds or retrained models?  
3. Do the overfitting patterns in deeper layers correlate with specific training phenomena (e.g., label noise, learning-rate schedule)?  
4. Have you experimented with freezing or regularizing layers based on convergence patterns, and if so, does this preserve clean accuracy or robustness?  
5. How do you expect your findings to extend to larger datasets (full ImageNet) or non-vision domains (e.g., NLP)?

Limitations:
The work is confined to ℓ∞ adversarial robustness on relatively small vision datasets using ResNet-based models.  Analyses are correlational and rely on a single primary similarity metric (CKA).  The paper does not address potential societal impacts of adversarially robust models, such as environmental costs or misuse.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept
The paper is technically solid and offers moderate-to-high impact insights into the representational geometry and learning dynamics of adversarially robust networks.  While there are no major flaws in evaluation or reproducibility, clarifications on scope, additional validation of similarity metrics, and statistical analyses would further strengthen the contribution.

The authors also analyze the similarity of representations when other perturbations are applied JPEG, Gabor, and Snow, finding

Such properties as the authors point out are not captured by aggregate performance metrics such as loss or accuracy, leading to insights about the learned representations. Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper provides a systematic investigation of how adversarially robust training reshapes the internal representations of deep neural networks through the lens of representation similarity. Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) is employed across layers of Wide ResNets on CIFAR-10 and ResNet-50s on ImageNette/ImageWoof, under varying model widths, training budgets, and threat models (e.g., PGD, TRADES, JPEG, Gabor, Snow). Key empirical findings include:  
- The familiar “block structure” of non-robust networks disappears in robust models, giving rise to unexpectedly high long-range similarity across distant layers.  
- Early layers of both robust and non-robust networks are relatively insensitive to adversarial perturbations, whereas deeper layers diverge sharply and exhibit overfitting during robust training.  
- Representations learned under different threat classes converge to remarkably similar patterns at low perturbation budgets, suggesting latent correspondences among seemingly disparate attacks.  
Based on these observations, the authors propose several design principles—such as layer-freezing schedules, layer-wise specialization regularizers, and principled threat-model selection for joint robustness—to guide future robust training strategies.

Strengths:
1. Representation-Similarity Perspective: Building on a few prior explorations of CKA in model comparison, this work delivers the first large-scale, layer-wise study focused squarely on adversarially robust vs. standard training. The identification of “salient pitfalls” (loss of specialization, deeper-layer overfitting, and cross-threat homogenization) is both novel and well motivated.  
2. Comprehensive Experimental Design: The authors evaluate across multiple datasets (CIFAR-10, ImageNette, ImageWoof), architectures (Wide ResNets, ResNet-50), perturbation budgets, and threat types. Ablation studies and cross-model analyses lend credibility to their core claims.  
3. Clarity and Organization: The manuscript is generally well written and follows a logical flow from background through methodology to results. Heatmaps of cross-layer CKA and quantitative curves effectively illustrate the dynamics of representation similarity. Code release further enhances reproducibility.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:
1. Reliance on a Single Similarity Metric: All analyses use CKA, which—while efficient—may overlook non-linear relationships or be sensitive to centering and batching choices. The authors should validate key findings with an alternative metric (e.g., PWCCA or Procrustes distance) to rule out CKA-specific artifacts.  
2. Limited Causal and Practical Validation: Although the paper articulates promising strategies (layer freezing, specialization regularizers, joint threat-model training), it stops short of experimentally demonstrating that these interventions yield tangible improvements in clean or robust accuracy. Incorporating pilot studies—for example, freezing early layers once their CKA convergence plateaus or applying a simple regularizer to maintain block structure—would strengthen the practical impact.  
3. Dataset and Architecture Scope: Experiments are confined to CIFAR-10 and small ImageNet subsets; it remains unclear if these representation dynamics hold on full ImageNet or non-convolutional architectures (e.g., vision transformers). Extending evaluations to larger benchmarks or other modalities (text, speech) would bolster the paper’s generality.  
4. Statistical Rigor and Methodological Detail: The manuscript would benefit from reporting error bars or confidence intervals for CKA scores and accuracy curves across multiple random seeds. Key hyperparameters—such as CKA centering procedures, batch sizes for similarity computation, and normalization of ε—should be documented in the main text rather than relegated to the appendix.  
5. Computational and Ethical Considerations: A brief discussion of the computational cost and potential environmental footprint of robust training (especially if new interventions increase training time) is warranted. Additionally, acknowledging possible misuse—such as crafting more transferable adversarial examples using these diagnostic tools—would present a balanced perspective on societal impact.

Questions for the Authors:
1. How sensitive are your principal observations (block-structure loss, deeper-layer overfitting, cross-threat similarity) to the choice of similarity metric?  
2. Have you tried any layer-freezing or targeted regularization schedules in practice, and if so, what effects did these have on clean/robust accuracy trade-offs?  
3. Would your conclusions extend to vision transformers or full ImageNet training?  
4. Can you provide variance estimates for CKA and accuracy metrics over multiple runs to assess statistical significance?  
5. How did you choose CKA centering and batching parameters, and how might alternative choices influence your findings?

Decision:
6: Weak Accept. This work is technically solid and offers moderate-to-high impact insights into representation dynamics under adversarial training. While the core analyses are convincing and well supported, the paper would benefit from additional validation of proposed interventions, broader evaluation across architectures and datasets, and stronger statistical reporting. With these enhancements, the study would provide an even more compelling foundation for future robust training methods.

reviews.summary:
This paper presents a probing analysis on clean (non-robust) vs. adversarially trained robust models. The paper's novelty is questionable and the insights gained are also sort of obvious, e.g., that the representational differences between inputs increase as one goes deeper down the layers. While this is true (and has been observed by the authors in their experiments), the authors didn't make an attempt to analyze whether two inputs from the same class are do have similar representations, which is a good thing. How does this compare between robust and non-robust models?

The paper introduces a lot of defence mechanisms (mainly centred around the min-max idea). However, it only employs PGD asdversarial training for obtaining a "robust" model. What about other approaches that provide defence mechanisms against potential attacks, e.g. the references 23, 32, 43 etc. that the authors themselves cite?
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

1. Good analysis work on robust vs. non-robust networks.
2. Uses CKA to measure representational similarities.

Weaknesses:

1. Only one version of robust network considered - those trained with PGD based adversarial examples.
2. The rational of using CKA is not appropriately justified.
3. Some analysis could/should have been at a more detailed level, like one would still want the differences between different classes to be high and similarties between identical classes to be high. What observations can we make regarding this expected behavior in a robust and a non-robust network?
reviews.questions: How do the analysis from the observational differences between robust and non-robust networks can actually be used in practice? Can we use these insights to guide us towards constructing more robust models?
reviews.limitations: Limitations are not properly explained. The expression "fundamental disjunct between aggregate properties and layer-wise representation similarity metrics..." is rather vague.
reviews.rating: 4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper presents a probing analysis on clean (non-robust) vs. adversarially trained robust models. The paper's novelty is questionable and the insights gained are also sort of obvious, e.g., that the representational differences between inputs increase as one goes deeper down the layers. While this is true (and has been observed by the authors in their experiments), the authors didn't make an attempt to analyze whether two inputs from the same class are do have similar representations, which is a good thing. How does this compare between robust and non-robust models?

The paper introduces a lot of defence mechanisms (mainly centred around the min-max idea). However, it only employs PGD asdversarial training for obtaining a "robust" model. What about other approaches that provide defence mechanisms against potential attacks, e.g. the references 23, 32, 43 etc. that the authors themselves cite?

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:

1. Good analysis work on robust vs. non-robust networks.
2. Uses CKA to measure representational similarities.

Weaknesses:

1. Only one version of robust network considered - those trained with PGD based adversarial examples.
2. The rational of using CKA is not appropriately justified.
3. Some analysis could/should have been at a more detailed level, like one would still want the differences between different classes to be high and similarties between identical classes to be high. What observations can we make regarding this expected behavior in a robust and a non-robust network?

Questions:
How do the analysis from the observational differences between robust and non-robust networks can actually be used in practice? Can we use these insights to guide us towards constructing more robust models?

Limitations:
Limitations are not properly explained. The expression "fundamental disjunct between aggregate properties and layer-wise representation similarity metrics..." is rather vague.

Rating:
4: Borderline reject: Technically solid paper where reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation, outweigh reasons to accept, e.g., good evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper undertakes a detailed layer-wise comparison of internal representations in clean (non-robust) versus adversarially trained (robust) models using centered kernel alignment (CKA). Experiments span CIFAR-10 and two ImageNet subsets (ImageNette, ImageWoof) with Wide ResNets and ResNet-50, and consider varying adversarial budgets and multiple perturbation types. Key empirical observations include the diminution of the familiar “block structure” in robust networks, elevated long-range similarity across layers, deeper-layer overfitting during robust training, and surprisingly high representational alignment across different threat models at low budgets. These diagnostic insights are positioned as a foundation for potential strategies such as layer freezing, layer-wise regularization, and informed multi-threat-model training.

Strengths:
1. Thorough empirical scope—The authors evaluate across multiple datasets, architectures, budgets, and threat models, supporting the robustness of their descriptive findings.  
2. Effective use of CKA—CKA enables an efficient, fine-grained comparison of layer representations, and the cross-layer heatmaps and quantitative analyses are clear and informative.  
3. Well organized presentation—The paper clearly motivates each analysis question, structures results around distinct phenomena (e.g., specialization loss, cross-threat similarity), and provides open-source code to facilitate reproducibility.  
4. Diagnostic value—The discovery of deeper-layer overfitting and cross-threat representational alignment offers novel angles for understanding the pathologies of robust training and suggests actionable research directions.

Weaknesses:
1. Questionable novelty and conceptual depth—While the descriptive phenomena (loss of block structure, higher long-range similarity) are interesting, they echo observations from prior work and lack a deeper theoretical framing or causal explanation.  
2. Single defense method—The study restricts “robust” models to PGD-based adversarial training (and TRADES in some cases), overlooking other defense mechanisms (e.g., references 23, 32, 43) that might exhibit different representational dynamics.  
3. Reliance on one similarity metric—Exclusively using CKA may overlook aspects of representation geometry. Alternative metrics such as PWCCA or Procrustes distance could validate whether these patterns are CKA-specific artifacts.  
4. Correlation without causality—All analyses are observational; the paper does not establish causal links between representational changes and performance trade-offs, nor does it empirically test whether proposed interventions (e.g., layer freezing, specialized regularizers) actually improve robustness or clean accuracy.  
5. Limited generality—Experiments are confined to CIFAR-10 and small ImageNet subsets. It remains unclear whether the findings extend to larger-scale vision tasks (full ImageNet) or non-visual domains (e.g., NLP, speech).  
6. Insufficient statistical rigor—The paper lacks variance estimates or confidence intervals for CKA scores and accuracy metrics, making it difficult to assess the statistical significance of the reported differences.  
7. Vague limitations and missing implications—The stated “fundamental disjunct between aggregate properties and layer-wise representation similarity metrics” is not well elaborated, and broader societal or environmental impacts of robust training (e.g., increased compute cost, potential misuse) are not discussed.

Suggestions for Improvement:
- Broaden the set of defense methods to include other adversarial training algorithms cited in the paper, and compare their representation dynamics.  
- Incorporate at least one complementary similarity metric (e.g., PWCCA) to verify that key observations are not artifacts of CKA.  
- Provide statistical analyses (e.g., error bars, multiple runs) for CKA measurements and accuracy to strengthen claims.  
- If feasible, implement small-scale interventions—such as early-layer freezing schedules or layer-wise regularizers based on CKA convergence—to demonstrate concrete performance benefits.  
- Clarify methodological details (CKA centering, batch sizes, epsilon normalization) to enhance reproducibility.  
- Expand the limitations section to discuss computational costs, potential ethical considerations, and the extent to which results might generalize beyond the datasets studied.

Questions for the Authors:
1. Have you evaluated whether the observed layer-wise phenomena persist under alternative similarity metrics (PWCCA, Procrustes)?  
2. Can you provide variance estimates or confidence intervals for your CKA scores across multiple training runs?  
3. Why focus exclusively on PGD/TRADES for robust training, and do you anticipate different representational outcomes with other defense schemes?  
4. Have you attempted preliminary layer-freezing or targeted regularization experiments to validate the practical value of your diagnostics?  
5. How sensitive are your findings to optimizer choice, learning-rate schedules, and data augmentation protocols?  
6. Can you comment on whether these insights extend to larger-scale datasets or non-vision modalities?

Rating:
4: Borderline reject. While the paper is technically solid and offers valuable descriptive insights into robust versus non-robust representations, the limited evaluation (single defense method, single similarity metric, small datasets) and lack of causal or practical follow-up dilute its impact. The reasons to reject (limited novelty, correlational scope, narrow generality) outweigh the paper’s strengths.

title: A theory of weight distribution-constrained learning

reviews.summary: The paper develops a theoretical framework for weight distribution-constrained perceptron learning using replica method. They compute capacity based on the proposed distribution, and develop a modification to SGD to satisfy the weight distribution constraints. Their theory is verified in simulations, and it allows to compute the optimal weight prior in the student-teacher learning case. Moreover, its predictions match the experimentally observed weight distributions in mouse primary auditory cortex.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
### Strengths
1. Capacity calculations are rigorous and in good agreement with simulations.
2. Student-teacher results on optimal priors are interesting and non-trivial.
3. The match with mouse data for E/I networks is really interesting, although it's not clear to me why the auditory cortex should look like a big perceptron. 

### Weaknesses
1. The results of this paper might be hard really to extend to multi-layer network (which is not surprising)
2. ...and also hard/tedious to extend to other distributions, as it would require a replica calculation (although some ground work for that is done in the appendix)

These are not crucial problems, however.

### Summary
I think it's an interesting paper with a solid theoretical results, good experiments and an interesting connection to real neural data; 8 (strong accept).

Lines 99-100: worth noting that the number of synapses per neuron is at most 1k-10k, depending on the area. The large N limit with many memories presumably makes the number of synapses infinite too. reviews.text:
Summary  
This paper presents a unified theoretical and algorithmic framework for perceptron learning under explicit constraints on the synaptic weight distribution. Building on Gardner’s replica‐symmetric calculation, the authors derive a closed‐form expression for the storage capacity of a perceptron whose weights must match an arbitrary target distribution. Remarkably, they show that the capacity loss relative to the unconstrained case scales with the squared Wasserstein‐2 distance between the target distribution’s CDF and that of a standard normal. To enforce distribution constraints in practice, they introduce DisCo‐SGD, an alternating procedure that combines standard SGD updates on the classification loss with one‐dimensional optimal‐transport (quantile‐matching) projections. They further interpret training trajectories as geodesic flows in Wasserstein space. In a teacher–student setting, the theory predicts an optimal prior that maximizes generalization performance, and these predictions are borne out in simulations. Finally, they apply their framework to excitatory/inhibitory lognormal synaptic distributions and find close agreement with weight‐distribution measurements from mouse primary auditory cortex.

Strengths  
1. Rigorous capacity analysis: The replica‐symmetric calculations are thorough and yield closed‐form capacity expressions that agree quantitatively with extensive simulations.  
2. Novel geometric insight: Relating capacity reduction to the squared Wasserstein distance offers a clear and original geometric interpretation not seen in prior perceptron work.  
3. DisCo‐SGD algorithm: The proposed alternating SGD–Wasserstein‐projection algorithm is well motivated, with training trajectories that empirically trace Wasserstein geodesics. This practical method bridges theory and algorithm.  
4. Teacher–student optimal prior: The extension to a student–teacher scenario yields non‐trivial predictions for optimal weight priors, improving generalization in simulations.  
5. Biological relevance: Applying the theory to E/I‐balanced lognormal distributions and matching mouse auditory cortex data provides a compelling connection between structural synaptic statistics and learning performance.  
6. Clarity and reproducibility: The manuscript is well organized, with detailed appendices presenting proofs and algorithmic details. Figures effectively illustrate key concepts, and code/data availability is clearly stated.

Weaknesses  
1. Replica‐symmetry assumption: All analytic results rely on the replica‐symmetric ansatz. While the authors test extreme binary limits in the appendix, a more systematic investigation of replica‐symmetry breaking (RSB) regimes—especially near bimodal or sparse distributions—would strengthen confidence in the theory.  
2. Finite‐size effects: The capacity and generalization analyses are presented in the large‐N limit without explicit finite‐size scaling or error‐bar reporting. Empirical quantification of deviations at moderate N would clarify practical applicability.  
3. Single‐layer, i.i.d. Gaussian inputs: The perceptron model with i.i.d. inputs limits direct applicability to deep or recurrent architectures and to structured or correlated data. Extensions to multi‐layer or non‐Gaussian inputs remain speculative.  
4. Computational cost and scalability: DisCo‐SGD requires computing and inverting empirical CDFs at each iteration. The manuscript lacks a runtime complexity analysis or benchmarks for large N, and guidance on mini‐batch or approximate transport maps is not provided.  
5. Hyperparameter sensitivity: Practical guidelines for choosing the two learning rates (classification vs. distribution steps), projection frequency, and other hyperparameters are not elaborated. Sensitivity analyses would aid practitioners.  
6. Biological interpretation: It is not clear why the entire mouse auditory cortex should behave like a single perceptron performing pure classification. A discussion of whether the data in [27] come from a specific subarea or functional motif, and the implications for applying perceptron theory to cortical circuits, is warranted.

Detailed Suggestions  
1. Replica‐symmetry breaking and finite‐N: Include numerical experiments (or cite relevant work) probing capacity predictions under RSB near strongly non‐Gaussian targets. Provide finite‐size scaling plots or error bars to quantify deviations from the large‐N theory at realistic synapse counts (1k–10k).  
2. Algorithmic complexity: Report per‐iteration computational cost of DisCo‐SGD (e.g., O(N) sorting/inversion) and discuss scalability to large‐scale or deep networks. Consider brief comments on approximate or mini‐batch CDF estimators.  
3. Hyperparameter guidance: Describe how the classification‐step learning rate η₁ and distribution‐projection step η₂ were chosen across experiments. Provide recommendations (or default values) for balancing convergence speed and distribution fidelity.  
4. Extensions to structured data: Discuss how correlated or non‐Gaussian inputs would alter the replica calculation and whether DisCo‐SGD requires modification for structured datasets.  
5. Biological context: In Section 5, elaborate on the choice of auditory cortex as a perceptron analogue. If the data derive from a subpopulation engaged in classification, note this; otherwise, clarify the interpretation and limitations.  
6. Sensitivity to mis‐specified priors: Investigate how small deviations in the target distribution q(w) affect capacity and generalization. This will inform the robustness of the optimal‐prior predictions.  

Minor Corrections  
– Table 1 is labeled as a table but functions as a figure—consider relabeling as “Figure 1.”  
– Lines 99–100: note that typical synapse counts per neuron (1 k–10 k) make N finite; comment on the large‐N assumption.  
– Line 121: reiterate that key formulas assume Var(w)=1.  
– Line 233: add parentheses around arccos arguments.  
– Line 241: capitalize “Gaussian.”  
– Remove appendix placeholders from the main PDF.  
– Appendix Figs. 5–6: correct “seperation” to “separation.”  
– Eq. 2: change λ → ρ.  
– Eq. 3: replace index i to avoid conflict with imaginary unit.  

Decision  
8: Strong Accept. This paper offers a technically robust and conceptually novel contribution by marrying replica‐based capacity analysis with optimal‐transport‐based distribution enforcement. Its combination of rigorous theory, a practical algorithm, non‐trivial teacher–student insights, and a compelling link to biological data make it a high‐impact work in statistical mechanics, computational neuroscience, and constrained‐optimization communities.

reviews.summary: The authors compare the capacity of a perceptron with normally distributed weights to the capacity of a perceptron with weights distributed according to a specified distribution. They show analytically that the difference in capacity is a function of the Wasserstein distance between the normal distribution and the specified distribution. Based on their analysis, the authors derive an algorithm for optimizing the weights of a perceptron subject to a specified weight distribution, which they analyze using tools from optimal transport theory. The authors consider a perceptron whose positive/negative weights are constrained to have log-normal distributions and show that the optimal parameters of the log-normal distributions are close to experimentally observed parameters from connectomics data. Finally, they apply their theory to a student-teacher network and compute the optimal prior on the student network weights given knowledge of the distribution of the teacher network weights.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
As far as I can tell, this work is original (though this is not my specific area of expertise) and their technical arguments are clear (though I did not read the appendices carefully).

My main concern is with the motivation of the paper. In the second paragraph of the introduction, the authors first cite work stating that near capacity, an unconstrained perceptron classifying random input-output associations has normally distributed weights. Then they cite work showing that connectomics data indicates that weight distributions are not normally distributed, but rather closer to log-normally distributed. From my point of view, the natural conclusion from these theoretical and experimental works is that the perceptron is the wrong model with which to understand the log-normal weight distribution. A potential question is then: "What is the simplest task that is optimized when the weights are log-normal?" However, the authors come to an alternative conclusion: "The discrepancy between theory and current experimental result... call for a theory of perceptron learning that incorporates the knowledge of global structural information." Why is this the natural conclusion? Are the authors assuming that the log-normal distribution of the weights is a constraint that is not due to the learning task? I think this needs to be clarified.

This being said, I believe the results are interesting and worthwhile from a theoretical perspective, but the connections to brain function are less clear.
reviews.questions:
Given the discrepancy between experimentally observed weight distributions and the theoretically optimal distribution for the perceptron, why is the natural problem (from the perspective of a computational neuroscientist) to understand the performance of the perceptron under a constrained weight distribution?

Does your learning algorithm suggest how a biological circuit would enforce the distribution constraint (specifically, the optimal transport map step)?

Is there prior work along these lines?

Minor questions:

Why are Eq (4) and Eq (5) equivalent? Because the moments of the distributions are fixed to 1? I think this could be clarified.

In Eq (7) and Algorithm 1, what is $\mu$ summed over? The current batch? It current reads more like GD than SGD.
reviews.limitations: I do not foresee any direct negative societal impact of their work.
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 2: You are willing to defend your assessment, but it is quite likely that you did not understand the central parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
